questions.question: Which query correctly returns the 2nd highest salary from an employees table?
options: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)
SELECT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1
SELECT MIN(salary) FROM employees
SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees
questions.correctAnswer: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)
questions.explanation: The query first finds the highest salary and then returns the max value below it, which is the 2nd highest salary.
questions.hint: Find the global max first, then filter below it.

questions.question: A table has 100K rows. You run SELECT * FROM table. What is the best immediate way to reduce query cost?
options: Add an index
Use LIMIT
Use WHERE to filter
Both B and C
questions.correctAnswer: Both B and C
questions.explanation: Limiting returned rows and filtering scanned rows both reduce cost immediately. Indexes help mostly when predicates are selective.

questions.question: What does this query return? SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) FROM orders WHERE status = 'cancelled';
options: Total cancelled orders
Number of unique users who cancelled at least one order
Number of users with any order
Number of duplicate cancelled orders
questions.correctAnswer: Number of unique users who cancelled at least one order
questions.explanation: COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) counts unique users, not order rows.

questions.question: Why does SQL survive across every new database and engine?
options: It solves a universal problem (querying structured data) simply enough to learn fast and powerfully enough at TB scale
Apache standards mandate SQL as the only acceptable query language for any production data platform globally
SQL is faster than every alternative — designers cannot create a competitive query language in any modern hardware
SQL is encrypted at the protocol level — alternatives cannot achieve the same security guarantees by default
questions.correctAnswer: It solves a universal problem (querying structured data) simply enough to learn fast and powerfully enough at TB scale
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: designers face 'invent new language or support SQL' — they almost always choose SQL because users already know it.

questions.question: What does it mean that SQL is a declarative language?
options: You describe the result; the database optimizer picks the execution plan from data it has and you do not
You describe each step in order; the database executes them exactly as written without any optimization
You write Python loops and SQL translates them automatically into a query plan for the engine
You describe the schema only — actual queries run through a separate procedural language with side effects
questions.correctAnswer: You describe the result; the database optimizer picks the execution plan from data it has and you do not
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: declarative = describe result, not steps. Optimizer often beats hand-written procedural code because it has data info you do not.

questions.question: Why is SQL the first language in this curriculum?
options: It works everywhere data lives in structured form — Snowflake/BigQuery/Spark/dbt all use it as the interface
It is the easiest language to learn so beginners can start without prior programming experience required
Apache standards require SQL before any other language for any data engineering training program globally
It is the only language Lithuanian regulators approve for any cross-border data engineering course always
questions.correctAnswer: It works everywhere data lives in structured form — Snowflake/BigQuery/Spark/dbt all use it as the interface
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: change DB (Snowflake → BigQuery), SQL transfers. Change tools (dbt vs custom Python), SQL becomes more central. Portability = first language.

questions.question: What does HydroAlpes' scenario consistency give you?
options: Familiarity with column names builds over time so mental energy goes to the new SQL concept each lesson
Apache certification — only certified scenario companies are approved for any production training program
Faster query execution — the database caches HydroAlpes' schema for repeated queries across lessons
Encrypted transport — HydroAlpes data flows through TLS to keep customer information private during course
questions.correctAnswer: Familiarity with column names builds over time so mental energy goes to the new SQL concept each lesson
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: by Module 4, you know meter_id/daily_kw/region without rereading. Familiarity frees mental energy for new concepts.

questions.question: Why do new data engineers sometimes skip the Silver layer at HydroAlpes?
options: Going Bronze → Gold directly works for small projects but creates problems at scale — Silver separates quality vs aggregation bugs
Bronze data is already validated by source systems so Silver duplicates that work needlessly across projects
Apache standards permit Bronze → Gold pipelines for any small project below 1M rows daily without review
Silver layer adds 30% to processing time — small teams skip it to meet sprint deadlines on tight schedules
questions.correctAnswer: Going Bronze → Gold directly works for small projects but creates problems at scale — Silver separates quality vs aggregation bugs
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: works until it doesn't. Wrong Gold number = data quality bug or aggregation bug? Silver separates them. Clean Silver → bug is in Gold.

questions.question: What is the small SQL vocabulary that covers most data engineering queries?
options: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, JOIN — recognize these and you can read most SQL
SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, DROP, ALTER — the seven core SQL statements for any platform
SELECT, FROM, IF, ELSE, FOR, WHILE, RETURN — combining declarative SELECT with imperative control flow
SELECT, FROM, LIKE, WHERE, BETWEEN, IN, EXCEPT — the seven keywords that handle 90% of analytical queries
questions.correctAnswer: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, JOIN — recognize these and you can read most SQL
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: small vocabulary. Course teaches each clause in its own lesson, building the full set one piece at a time.

questions.question: Why does `HAVING total_kw > 1000000` fail when `total_kw` is a SELECT alias?
options: HAVING runs at step 4 (before SELECT at step 5) — alias does not exist yet at HAVING evaluation time
HAVING cannot reference any column in the table — only literal values are allowed at HAVING evaluation
Apache SQL standard forbids alias usage in HAVING for portability across different database engines
HAVING runs in parallel with SELECT — they cannot share state due to concurrency in modern databases
questions.correctAnswer: HAVING runs at step 4 (before SELECT at step 5) — alias does not exist yet at HAVING evaluation time
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: execution order is FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY. Alias defined in step 5 not visible at step 4. Use SUM(daily_kw) directly.

questions.question: What proportion of SQL written by data engineers is portable across platforms?
options: Roughly 90% works on any platform unchanged; the remaining 10% needs minor adjustments (date functions, DDL)
Roughly 50% is portable — every platform has its own dialect with significant incompatibilities everywhere
0% — SQL is platform-specific from day one and engineers must learn each dialect from scratch always
100% — Apache SQL standard guarantees identical behavior across PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Spark
questions.correctAnswer: Roughly 90% works on any platform unchanged; the remaining 10% needs minor adjustments (date functions, DDL)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 90% standard SQL = productive on any platform immediately. Look up platform-specific functions (date, string, DDL) when encountered.

questions.question: If two SQL clients return different results for the same query, what is the most likely cause?
options: Different database connections (production vs development) — DBeaver/psql/Python all send identical SQL text
DBeaver has a different SQL parser than psql — clients silently rewrite queries before sending them to the engine
The Python script encrypts queries before sending — psql receives plaintext so the database returns different results
Apache PEP 8 specifies different result formatting per client — display rules differ across SQL client tools
questions.correctAnswer: Different database connections (production vs development) — DBeaver/psql/Python all send identical SQL text
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: clients only display results. Switching clients does not change query results. Different results = different databases (prod vs dev).

questions.question: What does Module 2 introduce after Module 1's foundation?
options: The structures SQL operates on — tables, columns, rows, types, primary/foreign keys, schemas
Apache Spark fundamentals — DataFrame APIs, lazy evaluation, partitioning, and the Catalyst optimizer
PostgreSQL administration — installation, user management, backups, and replication configuration
Window functions — OVER, PARTITION BY, ranking functions, and frame specifications for analytics
questions.correctAnswer: The structures SQL operates on — tables, columns, rows, types, primary/foreign keys, schemas
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Module 2 covers the relational model that SQL queries against. Tables, types, keys, relationships make every later concept concrete.

questions.question: Why is a table called 'a contract'?
options: Column definitions guarantee every row has the same structure and every value has the correct type
Apache TableContract specification governs every relational database's table format across all platforms
Each table has a legal contract attached — Lithuanian regulators require contracts for any production table
Tables sign cryptographically when modified — modifications produce a verifiable receipt with hash chain
questions.correctAnswer: Column definitions guarantee every row has the same structure and every value has the correct type
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: queries assume the data's shape because the database enforces it. CSV header has no enforcement; table column types do.

questions.question: What is the cost of storing `daily_kw` as VARCHAR instead of DOUBLE PRECISION?
options: No SUM without cast, broken `> 100` filtering ('9' > '100' as text), loss of all numeric optimizations
Slower writes only — reads are unaffected because PostgreSQL auto-casts text to numeric on SELECT always
Smaller storage size — VARCHAR is 30% smaller than DOUBLE PRECISION for typical numeric ranges in practice
Apache PEP 8 forbids it — but the database itself has no consequence beyond a deprecation warning at parse
questions.correctAnswer: No SUM without cast, broken `> 100` filtering ('9' > '100' as text), loss of all numeric optimizations
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: text comparison is alphabetical: '9' > '100' = TRUE. Numeric comparison: 9 > 100 = FALSE. Always use the correct type.

questions.question: What is the 'grain' of a table at HydroAlpes?
options: What one row represents — guides what the table measures, meaningful aggregations, expected row counts
The encryption granularity — which columns are encrypted versus stored in plaintext at rest in the table
The replication granularity — how many partitions the table has across the database's storage cluster
The compression granularity — how Parquet groups rows for compression purposes during the data write
questions.correctAnswer: What one row represents — guides what the table measures, meaningful aggregations, expected row counts
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: meter_readings grain = meter-day. 350K meters × 365 days = ~128M rows/year. Mental arithmetic guides every query against the table.

questions.question: What is the rule about primary keys in HydroAlpes' Silver and Gold tables?
options: Every Silver and Gold table must have a primary key; Bronze may lack keys because it stores raw data as-is
Every table must have a primary key regardless of layer — Apache standards require PKs in all tables
Only Bronze tables need primary keys — Silver and Gold derive their uniqueness from the Bronze layer
No tables need primary keys — modern data engineering uses unique indexes instead of PKs everywhere
questions.correctAnswer: Every Silver and Gold table must have a primary key; Bronze may lack keys because it stores raw data as-is
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: tables without PKs invite duplicates, prevent targeted updates, slow joins. Lena's rule: every Silver/Gold has a PK; Bronze raw data is exempt.

questions.question: What does a foreign key define between two tables?
options: A relationship — child references parent's primary key. Foundation for joins in Module 6 across all tables.
A read-only mirror — the child table is replicated from the parent at insertion time across the database
An encryption boundary — child data is encrypted with a key derived from the parent's primary key value
A partition rule — child rows are physically stored next to their parent rows for I/O performance only
questions.correctAnswer: A relationship — child references parent's primary key. Foundation for joins in Module 6 across all tables.
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: meter_readings.meter_id → meters.meter_id. One-to-many. Every join you write follows a relationship defined by an FK.

questions.question: What dual purpose do `CHECK` constraints serve at HydroAlpes?
options: Enforce business rules at insert time AND document data rules visibly in the schema definition itself
Encrypt the column with a per-check key AND replicate it to a separate audit table for compliance
Trigger Apache Airflow alerts AND log to the security team's centralized SIEM system on violation
Bypass the WAL AND prevent the DB from logging insert events — performance optimization for inserts
questions.correctAnswer: Enforce business rules at insert time AND document data rules visibly in the schema definition itself
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: CHECK (daily_kw >= 0) enforces + documents. Reader sees rule in schema without reading pipeline code or external docs months later.

questions.question: What does the schema name in `silver.meter_readings` signal at HydroAlpes?
options: Trust signal — gold = clean/validated/aggregated; bronze = raw/unvalidated. Reviewer assesses inputs without tracing pipeline.
Compression signal — gold uses zstd, silver uses snappy, bronze uses no compression at all by convention
Region signal — gold for Switzerland, silver for Austria, bronze for combined cross-border data sources
Replication signal — gold replicates 3×, silver replicates 2×, bronze stays on single-node storage only
questions.correctAnswer: Trust signal — gold = clean/validated/aggregated; bronze = raw/unvalidated. Reviewer assesses inputs without tracing pipeline.
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: convention. gold prefix = trust. Reviewer assesses input reliability by schema prefix without tracing full pipeline lineage.

questions.question: Why is Module 2 lesson 8 called 'the data model that you will return to frequently'?
options: Module 5 GROUP BY uses these column names; Module 6 joins use these FK relationships; Module 8 windows use these grains
Apache standards require students to bookmark Lesson 8 in any production SQL training course globally
The lesson contains hidden test questions that resurface in every module's quiz from Module 3 onward
It is the first lesson with a real-world example, so it serves as the primary code reference for the course
questions.correctAnswer: Module 5 GROUP BY uses these column names; Module 6 joins use these FK relationships; Module 8 windows use these grains
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: data model = foundation for every later query. M5 column names, M6 FK relationships, M8 grains. Bookmark and revisit.

questions.question: What does `WHERE substation_id = NULL` return?
options: Zero rows — NULL = NULL is NULL (unknown), never TRUE; WHERE keeps only TRUE rows so all NULLs are excluded
All NULL rows — Apache SQL standard treats `= NULL` as equivalent to `IS NULL` for backward compatibility
All non-NULL rows — `= NULL` matches any value because NULL represents 'any unknown value' in the spec
An error — modern PostgreSQL refuses to compile `= NULL` and requires `IS NULL` syntax at parse time
questions.correctAnswer: Zero rows — NULL = NULL is NULL (unknown), never TRUE; WHERE keeps only TRUE rows so all NULLs are excluded
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: NULL comparisons always produce NULL, not TRUE/FALSE. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL. Most common bug: query returns fewer rows than expected.

questions.question: What is the mental shift from Python row-by-row to SQL set-based thinking?
options: Describe the result; the database handles iteration. Operate on sets of columns/rows/conditions at once.
Loops first, then SQL — SQL is just a Python loop wrapper that executes faster on the database server
Always use cursors — SQL's row-by-row mode is faster than its set mode for any workload over 1M rows
Apache standards require set-based thinking only after 5 years of Python experience for proper grounding
questions.correctAnswer: Describe the result; the database handles iteration. Operate on sets of columns/rows/conditions at once.
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Module 3 onward operates on sets. SELECT chooses columns, FROM provides rows, WHERE selects subset. Set-based reasoning makes SQL powerful.

questions.question: What does every SELECT statement produce?
options: A result set — temporary table that exists for the duration of the query unless saved with CTAS
A permanent table — every SELECT auto-creates a backing table that requires manual cleanup later
An encrypted snapshot — Apache standards mandate encryption of every result set sent to clients always
A binary file — result sets are written to disk as Parquet files for retrieval by client applications
questions.correctAnswer: A result set — temporary table that exists for the duration of the query unless saved with CTAS
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: temporary result. Has its own columns and rows. Not stored anywhere unless explicitly saved (CTAS in Module 9).

questions.question: Why does selecting too many columns matter for pipeline queries?
options: Wastes resources and obscures intent — right set is the minimum that answers the question being asked
Speeds up queries — Apache standards say wider SELECT lists are 30% faster on PostgreSQL than narrow
Triggers Lithuanian regulator alerts — wide SELECT lists must be reported to the data protection officer
Generates UI artifacts — wide SELECT lists pre-populate dashboard tables faster than narrow ones do
questions.correctAnswer: Wastes resources and obscures intent — right set is the minimum that answers the question being asked
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: too many = waste + obscured intent. Too few = rewrite when requirements change. Right set = minimum that answers the question.

questions.question: When is `SELECT *` acceptable at HydroAlpes?
options: Ad-hoc exploration in a notebook — quick view of structure without listing every column manually
Production pipeline writes to Gold — atomic, simple, readable for downstream consumers in production
Inside a CTE referenced multiple times — minimizes repetition of long column lists across the query
Within a CTAS statement — the asterisk freezes column list at table creation time per Apache standards
questions.correctAnswer: Ad-hoc exploration in a notebook — quick view of structure without listing every column manually
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: ad-hoc OK. Pipeline = explicit columns (downstream breaks if upstream adds/renames columns). COUNT(*) is the exception (counts rows, not columns).

questions.question: Why can't you use a SELECT alias in the WHERE clause?
options: Execution order — SELECT runs at step 5; WHERE at step 2. Alias does not exist yet when WHERE evaluates
Apache SQL standard forbids alias-in-WHERE for portability across different database engines reliably
Alias performance — using aliases in WHERE forces full table scans per Apache PostgreSQL benchmarks
Alias scoping — aliases live in a separate namespace that WHERE cannot access without explicit import
questions.correctAnswer: Execution order — SELECT runs at step 5; WHERE at step 2. Alias does not exist yet when WHERE evaluates
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: WHERE evaluates at step 2 (before SELECT at step 5). Alias not yet defined. Use original expression. ORDER BY (step 6) can use aliases.

questions.question: What does an expression in SELECT (like `daily_kw * 0.001 AS daily_mw`) produce?
options: A new computed column in the result set — never modifies the stored data in the source table
A new column added to the source table permanently — Apache standards require rollback to undo it
A new index on the original column — auto-created when expressions reference it in any SELECT
A new partition — the database splits the table by the expression's distinct values automatically
questions.correctAnswer: A new computed column in the result set — never modifies the stored data in the source table
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: SELECT expressions never modify stored data. Experiment freely with conversions, formatting, calculations. Source table untouched.

questions.question: Why is DISTINCT expensive on large result sets?
options: Database must sort or hash the entire result set to identify duplicates — memory and processing intensive
Apache PostgreSQL has a hard limit of 1M rows for DISTINCT — larger sets are silently truncated to 1M
DISTINCT triggers Lithuanian regulator audits — every DISTINCT execution must be logged for compliance
DISTINCT requires recompiling the query plan every time — overhead is fixed regardless of data size
questions.correctAnswer: Database must sort or hash the entire result set to identify duplicates — memory and processing intensive
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: sort/hash = expensive on millions of rows. If PK guarantees uniqueness, DISTINCT is wasted. Use intentionally for unique-value questions.

questions.question: What is the standard exploratory query pattern at HydroAlpes?
options: SELECT * FROM schema.table LIMIT 10 — quick view of structure, types, NULLs, shape without loading millions
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM schema.table — row count first, then load full table for inspection in chunks
DESCRIBE schema.table — Apache PostgreSQL standard for inspecting table structure programmatically
SHOW INDEXES schema.table — index inspection is the first step before any exploration of new tables
questions.correctAnswer: SELECT * FROM schema.table LIMIT 10 — quick view of structure, types, NULLs, shape without loading millions
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: every new table → SELECT * LIMIT 10. Column names + types + sample values + NULL presence + general shape in one query.

questions.question: What should SQL comments document?
options: Business logic, design decisions, non-obvious reasons — the why, not the what
Every SELECT clause — SQL is unreadable without per-line comments per Apache PEP 8 standards globally
Engineer name and date — required by Lithuanian regulators for any production query change always
The query plan — paste EXPLAIN ANALYZE output as a comment so reviewers see the optimizer's choices
questions.correctAnswer: Business logic, design decisions, non-obvious reasons — the why, not the what
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 'filter to RESIDENTIAL' on WHERE customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' adds nothing. 'billing rates differ by customer type' explains why. Comment why.

questions.question: Why does HydroAlpes' style guide require table qualification (`alias.column`) even for single-table queries?
options: A single-table query may become a join later — qualifying from the start prevents ambiguity errors during change
Apache PEP 8 standard mandates qualification — unqualified columns fail PostgreSQL strict-mode validation
Qualified columns run 20% faster — the parser skips disambiguation logic when columns are explicitly qualified
Lithuanian regulators audit unqualified columns — every unqualified reference must be logged for compliance
questions.correctAnswer: A single-table query may become a join later — qualifying from the start prevents ambiguity errors during change
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: future-proofing. Original query unqualified → adding join introduces ambiguity errors → must rewrite all column refs. Qualifying prevents.

questions.question: What does Module 4 add to Module 3's SELECT toolkit?
options: WHERE for row filtering and ORDER BY for sorting — precise question = SELECT cols + WHERE rows + ORDER BY order
JOIN for combining tables — SELECT only handles single-table queries until Module 6 introduces joins
GROUP BY for aggregation — SELECT only returns raw rows until Module 5 introduces grouping
INSERT for writing — SELECT only reads until Module 9 introduces data modification statements
questions.correctAnswer: WHERE for row filtering and ORDER BY for sorting — precise question = SELECT cols + WHERE rows + ORDER BY order
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: M3 chooses columns. M4 chooses rows + sort order. 'Show me X for Y sorted by Z' = SELECT + WHERE + ORDER BY in three module-3-and-4 lessons.

questions.question: What is the rule about quotes in SQL value comparisons?
options: Single quotes for text/date values; double quotes are reserved for identifiers (column/table names) in PostgreSQL
Double quotes for everything — single quotes are deprecated in modern SQL since the 2016 standard
Backticks for everything — Apache standards prefer backticks for portability across MySQL/Postgres alike
No quotes — Apache SQL infers types automatically and quotes are decorative whitespace per spec rules
questions.correctAnswer: Single quotes for text/date values; double quotes are reserved for identifiers (column/table names) in PostgreSQL
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: WHERE region = 'BERN' (single quotes for value). WHERE region = "BERN" (double quotes treats BERN as a column name → 'column not found' error).

questions.question: What is the most common typo for Python developers learning SQL?
options: Using `==` instead of `=` for equality — Python uses `==`, SQL uses `=` for both equality and aliasing
Using `=` instead of `==` for assignment — SQL has no assignment so the typo always fails noisily
Using `IS` instead of `=` for value equality — Python's `is` checks identity but SQL's IS is for NULL only
Using `LIKE` instead of `=` for exact matches — Python has no LIKE operator so this is a fresh concept
questions.correctAnswer: Using `==` instead of `=` for equality — Python uses `==`, SQL uses `=` for both equality and aliasing
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: SQL has no `==`. WHERE region == 'BERN' produces a syntax error. Python double-equals is the most common transition typo.

questions.question: Why is `WHERE region = 'BERN' OR region = 'ZURICH' AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'` a precedence trap?
options: AND has higher precedence than OR — DB reads as 'BERN OR (ZURICH AND INDUSTRIAL)'. Use parens to group OR first.
OR has higher precedence than AND — DB reads as '(BERN OR ZURICH) AND INDUSTRIAL'. Add parens around OR.
AND and OR have equal precedence — DB evaluates left-to-right and produces 'BERN AND ZURICH OR INDUSTRIAL'
AND/OR precedence varies by database — Apache standards say 'always use parens' but don't specify the order
questions.correctAnswer: AND has higher precedence than OR — DB reads as 'BERN OR (ZURICH AND INDUSTRIAL)'. Use parens to group OR first.
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: AND binds tighter. (BERN OR ZURICH) AND INDUSTRIAL ≠ BERN OR (ZURICH AND INDUSTRIAL). Silent wrong results = parens make intent explicit.

questions.question: When is NOT clearer than the equivalent positive condition?
options: Paired with set operators: NOT IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH'), NOT BETWEEN, NOT LIKE — reads naturally as 'not in set'
Paired with all comparison operators — NOT > , NOT <, NOT = read more naturally than the inverse operators
Paired with NULL conditions — NOT NULL is clearer than IS NOT NULL per Apache PEP 8 styling guidelines
Paired with column names — NOT region reads more naturally than the equivalent comparison expressions
questions.correctAnswer: Paired with set operators: NOT IN ('BERN', 'ZURICH'), NOT BETWEEN, NOT LIKE — reads naturally as 'not in set'
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: NOT IN reads as 'not in the set'. NOT BETWEEN reads as 'outside the range'. NOT LIKE reads as 'doesn't match'. Clearer than inverse comparisons.

questions.question: Why does `BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31'` on a TIMESTAMP miss the last day's data?
options: '2025-01-31' is interpreted as '2025-01-31 00:00:00' — anything after midnight on Jan 31 is excluded
BETWEEN is exclusive on the upper bound — Apache standards make BETWEEN excludes the right endpoint
BETWEEN ignores time of day — Apache PostgreSQL truncates the right endpoint to midnight automatically
BETWEEN requires explicit timezones — without UTC, the date interpretation drifts by hours each invocation
questions.correctAnswer: '2025-01-31' is interpreted as '2025-01-31 00:00:00' — anything after midnight on Jan 31 is excluded
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: TIMESTAMP date-only literal = midnight. To include all of Jan 31, use < '2025-02-01' (half-open) or '2025-01-31 23:59:59'. DATE type avoids this.

questions.question: Why does `LIKE '%Alpine%'` (leading wildcard) force a full table scan?
options: Index cannot help when the pattern starts with an unknown sequence — DB must scan every row to find matches
Apache PostgreSQL disables indexes on LIKE patterns by default — full-text search is always preferred
Leading wildcards encrypt the search pattern — DB must decrypt every row to compare correctly
Lithuanian regulators forbid indexed wildcards — full scans are required for any LIKE query in production
questions.correctAnswer: Index cannot help when the pattern starts with an unknown sequence — DB must scan every row to find matches
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: indexes are sorted by prefix. '%Alpine%' has unknown prefix → cannot use index. For document search, use full-text features (tsvector, rlike).

questions.question: What is the pattern for filtering on a nullable column at HydroAlpes?
options: Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL; for `<>` or `NOT IN`, decide whether to include NULLs and add `OR col IS NULL`
Always cast to TEXT — converting NULL to 'NULL' string makes equality comparisons work correctly always
Always use COALESCE — wrap every column access in COALESCE(col, default) to eliminate NULL handling
Apache standards say 'NULLs auto-handle' — modern PostgreSQL treats NULL as default value silently
questions.correctAnswer: Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL; for `<>` or `NOT IN`, decide whether to include NULLs and add `OR col IS NULL`
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: nullable column + every WHERE must explicitly account for NULL. <> on NULL silently drops rows. Add OR col IS NULL when needed.

questions.question: What is the rule about implicit row order at HydroAlpes?
options: Never rely on it — without ORDER BY, order can change between executions, restarts, plan changes
Always insertion order — Apache PostgreSQL guarantees rows return in the order they were inserted
Always sorted by PK — every query without ORDER BY returns rows ordered by primary key automatically
Always physical order — rows return in the order they sit on disk regardless of query semantics
questions.correctAnswer: Never rely on it — without ORDER BY, order can change between executions, restarts, plan changes
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: DB makes no promise. Pipeline correctness depending on row order = ORDER BY mandatory. Implicit order can change anytime.

questions.question: Why does a query that filters to 10K rows then sorts run faster than one that sorts 128M then filters?
options: WHERE before ORDER BY (execution order) — filtering 128M → 10K means sort operates on 10K, not 128M
Apache PostgreSQL caches WHERE results in memory — ORDER BY reads from cache instead of disk always
Sorts are O(1) on filtered data — Apache standards optimize post-filter sorts to constant time complexity
Lithuanian regulators require WHERE-first execution — full sorts are blocked unless explicitly requested
questions.correctAnswer: WHERE before ORDER BY (execution order) — filtering 128M → 10K means sort operates on 10K, not 128M
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: WHERE step 2, ORDER BY step 6. DB filters first → sort gets reduced data set. Adding WHERE often makes slow queries dramatically faster.

questions.question: What is the data quality query pattern (`WHERE daily_kw < 0`)?
options: Range validation — find values outside expected bounds; signals sensor errors or pipeline bugs
Apache standards quality check — every Silver pipeline must SELECT WHERE col < 0 before any insert
Lithuanian regulator requirement — every Silver pipeline must report negative values to compliance team
Performance test — negative-value queries trigger the optimizer's slow path for benchmark calibration
questions.correctAnswer: Range validation — find values outside expected bounds; signals sensor errors or pipeline bugs
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: WHERE conditions = data quality checks. Negative consumption = sensor error. Out-of-range = pipeline bug. M5 adds duplicate detection with HAVING.

questions.question: Why does Lesson 1 say aggregation turns 'data into information'?
options: Raw rows are data; aggregated results are information — decisions made from totals, trends, averages
Apache standards classify aggregation output as information level 2 — raw data is level 1 always
Encryption changes data to information — aggregated data is encrypted while raw data is plaintext
Compression changes data to information — aggregated data uses Parquet, raw data uses CSV always
questions.correctAnswer: Raw rows are data; aggregated results are information — decisions made from totals, trends, averages
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: nobody decides on one meter's reading for one day. Decisions on regional totals, monthly trends, customer averages. Aggregation = decisions.

questions.question: What is the difference between `COUNT(*)` and `COUNT(column)`?
options: COUNT(*) counts all rows regardless of NULLs; COUNT(column) skips rows where that column is NULL
COUNT(*) is faster than COUNT(column) by 10× per Apache PostgreSQL benchmarks across all data sets
COUNT(*) requires Apache certification; COUNT(column) is the default in non-certified builds always
COUNT(*) ignores DISTINCT; COUNT(column) treats every value as distinct automatically by design
questions.correctAnswer: COUNT(*) counts all rows regardless of NULLs; COUNT(column) skips rows where that column is NULL
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: COUNT(*) = all rows. COUNT(col) = non-NULL values. Writing COUNT(col) when meaning COUNT(*) silently produces lower number on NULL rows.

questions.question: What is the relationship between AVG, SUM, and COUNT?
options: AVG(col) = SUM(col) / COUNT(col) using the non-NULL count; ≠ SUM(col) / COUNT(*)
AVG(col) = SUM(col) / COUNT(*) — the all-rows count is always the denominator regardless of NULLs
AVG(col) = arithmetic mean of all stored values including NULL treated as zero by default at runtime
AVG(col) = median of values — SQL's AVG is statistical median per Apache PostgreSQL implementation
questions.correctAnswer: AVG(col) = SUM(col) / COUNT(col) using the non-NULL count; ≠ SUM(col) / COUNT(*)
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: AVG uses non-NULL count. For all-rows average, write SUM(col) / COUNT(*) explicitly. Makes denominator choice visible.

questions.question: Why is reporting MIN, MAX, AVG together more informative than AVG alone?
options: Spread (MIN to MAX) often reveals more than average — 0.00 to 2,847.30 shows vacant + heavy industry diversity
Apache standards require reporting all three — single-metric reports fail compliance audits routinely
MIN+MAX+AVG together compute faster than AVG alone — DB reuses the index scan across all three
Lithuanian regulators audit reports for completeness — single-metric reports trigger a compliance review
questions.correctAnswer: Spread (MIN to MAX) often reveals more than average — 0.00 to 2,847.30 shows vacant + heavy industry diversity
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: AVG 1.46 hides diversity. MIN/MAX of 0.00/2,847.30 = 2,847x range = vacant buildings + heavy industry coexist. Spread reveals what AVG hides.

questions.question: Why does adding `meter_id` to GROUP BY change the result count?
options: 350K meters × distinct meter_id = 350K groups instead of 4 region groups — granularity changed
GROUP BY meter_id is forbidden by Apache standards — only categorical columns may appear in GROUP BY
meter_id is a PRIMARY KEY — adding it to GROUP BY triggers automatic rejection from PostgreSQL parser
Lithuanian regulators forbid grouping by PII columns — meter_id is PII so the query fails compliance
questions.correctAnswer: 350K meters × distinct meter_id = 350K groups instead of 4 region groups — granularity changed
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: silently silences error but changes meaning. region groups = 4. + meter_id = 350K. Think about desired groups before adding columns.

questions.question: How do you decide GROUP BY granularity at HydroAlpes?
options: Match the question being asked — CEO dashboard 4 rows (region only); billing 372 rows (region+type+date)
Always group by every column — finer granularity is always better for downstream consumer flexibility
Always group by one column — coarser granularity is required by Apache standards for performance
Group by the columns with indexes — only indexed columns may appear in GROUP BY per Apache standards
questions.correctAnswer: Match the question being asked — CEO dashboard 4 rows (region only); billing 372 rows (region+type+date)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: granularity = consumer's question. Fewer cols = big-picture (4 rows). More cols = detailed breakdown (372). Choose based on the question.

questions.question: Why is `WHERE region = 'BERN'` better than `HAVING region = 'BERN'`?
options: WHERE filters rows before grouping = less work. HAVING groups all rows then discards 3/4 = wasted work
WHERE supports indexes; HAVING does not — Apache PostgreSQL only uses indexes in WHERE clauses always
HAVING is forbidden for row-level conditions — Apache standards require WHERE for non-aggregate filters
Lithuanian regulators audit HAVING usage — only aggregate-level filters may appear in HAVING by law
questions.correctAnswer: WHERE filters rows before grouping = less work. HAVING groups all rows then discards 3/4 = wasted work
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: WHERE = row filter (before grouping). HAVING = group filter (after aggregation). HAVING for non-agg = correct but wasteful.

questions.question: How does NULL behave in GROUP BY?
options: NULLs form their own group — separate row in the result, count separately from non-NULL values
NULLs are excluded — GROUP BY skips rows where the grouping column is NULL automatically by default
NULLs cause GROUP BY to fail — Apache standards forbid grouping on nullable columns without coalesce
NULLs are treated as zero — GROUP BY converts NULL to 0 before grouping for numeric columns automatically
questions.correctAnswer: NULLs form their own group — separate row in the result, count separately from non-NULL values
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: NULLs form own group. SUM(daily_kw) with NULL → adds 500 NULL meters to its own row. NULLs are visible (not silently dropped).

questions.question: What is the standard analytical query template?
options: WHERE (filter rows) → GROUP BY (group) → SUM/AVG/COUNT (aggregate) → HAVING (filter groups) → ORDER BY (sort)
GROUP BY → WHERE → HAVING → ORDER BY → SUM/AVG → SELECT — Apache standard execution order in production
ORDER BY → WHERE → SUM → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT — optimal order per Apache PostgreSQL benchmarks
SUM → COUNT → GROUP BY → ORDER BY → WHERE → HAVING — query plan order in modern PostgreSQL versions
questions.correctAnswer: WHERE (filter rows) → GROUP BY (group) → SUM/AVG/COUNT (aggregate) → HAVING (filter groups) → ORDER BY (sort)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: every analytical query follows this template. Specific cols/conditions/functions change; structure stays. Internalize once, write any aggregation.

questions.question: What does Module 6 add to aggregation skills?
options: Joins — combining data from multiple tables. Aggregation + joins = complete foundation of analytical SQL
Window functions — running totals and moving averages without collapsing rows like GROUP BY does
DDL — creating tables to store aggregated results for downstream consumers across the data platform
Recursive queries — hierarchical aggregation across parent-child relationships in the data model
questions.correctAnswer: Joins — combining data from multiple tables. Aggregation + joins = complete foundation of analytical SQL
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Module 5 = single-table aggregation. M6 = joins. Joins + aggregation = analytical SQL. M8 = window functions. M9 = creating tables.

questions.question: Why are tables stored separately and joined when needed?
options: Normalization stores each fact once — joins reconstruct combined view without redundancy/inconsistency/storage waste
Apache standards mandate one table per concept — combined tables fail PostgreSQL strict-mode validation
Lithuanian regulators forbid combined tables — each entity must live in a separate table for audit
Combined tables are 10× slower than joined queries — Apache PostgreSQL optimizes joins better than scans
questions.correctAnswer: Normalization stores each fact once — joins reconstruct combined view without redundancy/inconsistency/storage waste
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: flat = redundancy + inconsistency + storage waste. Normalized + joins = each fact stored once. Joins reconstruct combined view when needed.

questions.question: What does INNER JOIN do silently that pipelines must guard against?
options: Drops unmatched rows without warning — 350K → 349,500 rows means 500 had meter_ids not in meters table
Sorts the result by the join key — Apache standards require this and it cannot be changed by users
Logs every dropped row to the audit log — Lithuanian regulators require this audit for compliance always
Caches the result for 24 hours — repeated INNER JOIN executions return the cached version automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Drops unmatched rows without warning — 350K → 349,500 rows means 500 had meter_ids not in meters table
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: INNER drops unmatched. Most common join data quality issue. Always verify row counts before/after, especially in production pipelines.

questions.question: What is HydroAlpes' default join type for Silver-to-Gold pipelines?
options: LEFT JOIN — preserves all source rows; missing matches show NULLs (visible) instead of silently dropped
INNER JOIN — drops unmatched rows automatically per Apache standards for data quality enforcement
FULL OUTER JOIN — Apache best practice for any production pipeline; preserves all rows from both sides
CROSS JOIN — produces every combination; default for any data engineering pipeline at scale globally
questions.correctAnswer: LEFT JOIN — preserves all source rows; missing matches show NULLs (visible) instead of silently dropped
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: LEFT JOIN preserves all left rows. Missing matches = NULLs (visible). Filter NULLs later if INNER behavior needed. Recovering from INNER = rewrite.

questions.question: What is the approximate distribution of join types in production data engineering?
options: LEFT ~60%, INNER ~30%, FULL OUTER ~8%, RIGHT < 2% — master LEFT and INNER for most production queries
INNER ~70%, LEFT ~25%, RIGHT ~3%, FULL OUTER ~2% — INNER dominates because it enforces match quality
FULL OUTER ~50%, INNER ~30%, LEFT ~15%, RIGHT ~5% — defensive joining is the modern default everywhere
Equal across all four — Apache standards recommend rotating join types for query plan diversity always
questions.correctAnswer: LEFT ~60%, INNER ~30%, FULL OUTER ~8%, RIGHT < 2% — master LEFT and INNER for most production queries
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: master LEFT + INNER = handle vast majority. FULL OUTER = data quality / reconciliation scripts. RIGHT = almost never the best choice.

questions.question: What is the safe pattern for CROSS JOIN at HydroAlpes?
options: Build scaffold by CROSS JOIN of small dimensions, then LEFT JOIN with large fact tables to find gaps
Always CROSS JOIN large tables — Apache PostgreSQL optimizes CROSS JOIN faster than other join types
Avoid CROSS JOIN entirely — Apache standards forbid CROSS JOIN in any production query without exception
CROSS JOIN must be replaced by FULL OUTER JOIN — Lithuanian regulators audit CROSS JOIN usage daily
questions.correctAnswer: Build scaffold by CROSS JOIN of small dimensions, then LEFT JOIN with large fact tables to find gaps
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 4 regions × 31 dates = 124 rows (fine). 350K meters × 365 days = 127.75M rows (overwhelming). Small dim × small dim = scaffold; LEFT JOIN with fact.

questions.question: Why does a self-join require aliases?
options: Same table appears twice in FROM — DB cannot distinguish which instance without aliases like 'today' and 'yesterday'
Apache SQL standard requires aliases for any join — even single-table queries must alias for portability
Aliases speed self-joins by 30% — Apache PostgreSQL caches aliased table references between query plans
Aliases are required only when the join key is a primary key — non-PK self-joins do not need aliases
questions.correctAnswer: Same table appears twice in FROM — DB cannot distinguish which instance without aliases like 'today' and 'yesterday'
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: SELECT meter_id FROM meter_readings JOIN meter_readings ON ... = ambiguous. FROM mr AS today JOIN mr AS yesterday = unambiguous.

questions.question: What is the difference between filter in ON vs filter in WHERE for LEFT JOIN?
options: ON filters right side before joining (preserves LEFT JOIN); WHERE on right column converts to INNER JOIN behavior
ON and WHERE are equivalent for LEFT JOIN — Apache SQL standard treats them identically in all cases
WHERE is faster than ON for LEFT JOIN — Apache PostgreSQL optimizer prefers WHERE for filter pushdown
ON is forbidden in LEFT JOIN by Apache standards — WHERE is the only correct location for filter conditions
questions.correctAnswer: ON filters right side before joining (preserves LEFT JOIN); WHERE on right column converts to INNER JOIN behavior
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: WHERE ep.price > 50 excludes NULL ep rows = excludes unmatched left rows = effectively INNER JOIN. Move filter to ON to preserve LEFT JOIN.

questions.question: What does HydroAlpes' style guide require for column references in joins?
options: Qualify every column with alias.column even when unambiguous — prevents breaks when new columns added
Use unqualified columns when possible — qualified columns are 20% slower per Apache PostgreSQL benchmarks
Use USING clauses everywhere — Apache standards require USING for any column-name-matching join always
Use NATURAL JOIN — auto-discovers shared columns and joins them per Apache portability conventions
questions.correctAnswer: Qualify every column with alias.column even when unambiguous — prevents breaks when new columns added
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: qualify everything = doesn't break when new columns added to either table. Data lineage immediately visible without checking schema.

questions.question: What does a multi-table join's row count reveal about relationships?
options: More rows than original fact table = one-to-many in the chain creates duplicates. Verify after every complex join.
Apache standards require row counts to be identical — anything else fails the post-join validation always
More rows is always better — Apache PostgreSQL optimizer prefers larger result sets for downstream caching
Row counts cannot change in joins — Apache SQL spec guarantees identical row counts before and after
questions.correctAnswer: More rows than original fact table = one-to-many in the chain creates duplicates. Verify after every complex join.
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: many-to-one (FK lookup) preserves count. One-to-many creates duplicates. Always verify row count after complex joins matches expectation.

questions.question: What does Module 7 build on top of joins?
options: Subqueries and CTEs — composing complex logic by nesting queries, with patterns previewed in M6 (gap detection, latest record)
Window functions — replacing all joins with OVER clauses for the analytical use cases that required joins
Stored procedures — wrapping joins in named procedures so consumers do not need to write joins themselves
Materialized views — automatically caching join results for downstream consumers of analytical queries
questions.correctAnswer: Subqueries and CTEs — composing complex logic by nesting queries, with patterns previewed in M6 (gap detection, latest record)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: gap detection scaffold + latest-record subquery in M6 = preview of subqueries. M7 formalizes: scalar/EXISTS/correlated/recursive CTE.

questions.question: What is the value of running a subquery independently before composing it?
options: Every subquery is a complete valid query on its own — debug by running each piece separately to find issues
Apache PostgreSQL caches subquery results — running independently warms the cache for the outer query
Subqueries run in parallel only after independent execution — outer queries serialize them otherwise
Lithuanian regulators require independent execution as the audit baseline before any composition allowed
questions.correctAnswer: Every subquery is a complete valid query on its own — debug by running each piece separately to find issues
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: build inside out, test each piece, then compose. Wrong outer result = run each subquery independently to find which is wrong.

questions.question: Why does `WHERE daily_kw > (SELECT daily_kw FROM ... WHERE region = 'BERN')` fail?
options: Subquery returns 90,000 rows; > operator cannot compare against many values — aggregate (AVG/MAX) or use IN
Subquery cannot reference daily_kw in WHERE — Apache standards forbid same-column subqueries in production
Subquery requires explicit LIMIT 1 — Apache PostgreSQL refuses unbounded subqueries in scalar context always
Subquery must be wrapped in EXISTS — Apache standards prohibit raw scalar subqueries without EXISTS wrapper
questions.correctAnswer: Subquery returns 90,000 rows; > operator cannot compare against many values — aggregate (AVG/MAX) or use IN
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: scalar subquery must return one value for > comparison. Aggregate (AVG/MAX/MIN) → one value, or use IN against a set. Error signals rethink.

questions.question: What must every subquery in the FROM clause have?
options: An alias — FROM (...) AS totals — without it, the DB has no name to reference the derived table's columns
An ORDER BY clause — Apache PostgreSQL requires explicit ordering of derived tables for determinism always
A LIMIT clause — Apache standards cap derived tables at 10,000 rows for performance reasons globally
A CTE wrapper — Apache standards forbid raw subqueries in FROM and require WITH-style declarations only
questions.correctAnswer: An alias — FROM (...) AS totals — without it, the DB has no name to reference the derived table's columns
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: derived table alias required. Choose descriptive aliases (totals, daily_stats) — not generic (t1, sub). Tells reader what derived table contains.

questions.question: Why can `NOT IN (subquery)` return zero rows when you expect many?
options: Any NULL in the subquery's result makes NOT IN produce no rows for any outer row — NULL trap
NOT IN requires LIMIT 1 — Apache PostgreSQL refuses unbounded NOT IN subqueries in production always
NOT IN cannot include CTEs — Apache standards forbid CTE-based subqueries inside NOT IN expressions
NOT IN converts to EXISTS automatically — Apache PostgreSQL silently rewrites and may produce empty sets
questions.correctAnswer: Any NULL in the subquery's result makes NOT IN produce no rows for any outer row — NULL trap
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: NULL with NOT IN = NULL (unknown), WHERE keeps only TRUE rows. Filter NULL in subquery (WHERE col IS NOT NULL) or use NOT EXISTS (NULL-safe).

questions.question: Why is NOT EXISTS preferred over NOT IN for anti-joins at HydroAlpes?
options: EXISTS checks existence of rows, not values — NULLs cannot cause unexpected behavior that plagues NOT IN
NOT IN is deprecated — Apache standards removed NOT IN from the spec in 2018 and EXISTS replaced it
EXISTS runs 10× faster — Apache PostgreSQL optimizes EXISTS through index lookup paths exclusively
Lithuanian regulators forbid NOT IN — Apache standards mandate NOT EXISTS for cross-border data flows
questions.correctAnswer: EXISTS checks existence of rows, not values — NULLs cannot cause unexpected behavior that plagues NOT IN
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: EXISTS = existence test, never compares values. NULLs in either table cannot cause NOT IN's NULL trap. Lena's style guide: NOT EXISTS for anti-join.

questions.question: What should you check before writing a correlated subquery?
options: Can it be done with JOIN+GROUP BY or window function (M8)? Either alternative is usually preferable
Is it Apache-certified? — Apache standards require certification for any correlated subquery in production
Is the outer query a CTE? — Apache PostgreSQL forbids correlated subqueries outside CTE-wrapped queries
Are both tables in the same schema? — Apache standards forbid cross-schema correlated subqueries always
questions.correctAnswer: Can it be done with JOIN+GROUP BY or window function (M8)? Either alternative is usually preferable
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: correlated = expensive. Ask: JOIN + GROUP BY? Window function? Either alternative usually preferable. HydroAlpes: correlated mainly in small-data quality scripts.

questions.question: Why have CTEs largely replaced nested subqueries in modern data engineering?
options: Top-down readable, named intermediate results, multiple references possible — dbt is built entirely on CTEs
CTEs are 10× faster than subqueries — Apache PostgreSQL optimizes CTE materialization per the 2020 standard
CTEs are Apache-certified — Apache standards mandate CTEs for any production query in cross-border data
Subqueries are deprecated — Apache standards removed nested subqueries from the SQL spec in 2018 onward
questions.correctAnswer: Top-down readable, named intermediate results, multiple references possible — dbt is built entirely on CTEs
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: CTEs since SQL:1999 standard. Top-down readable. Named intermediates. Re-referenced. dbt = built around CTEs (every dbt model = SELECT statement).

questions.question: What is HydroAlpes' rule about CTE responsibility?
options: Each CTE does one thing (filter, aggregate, join, compute); split when more than one GROUP BY or JOIN inside
Each CTE must aggregate — Apache standards forbid pure-filter CTEs in any production query globally
Each CTE must reference all upstream tables — Apache PostgreSQL fails CTE chains that skip levels
Each CTE must have at least 100 lines of comments — Apache documentation standards require this always
questions.correctAnswer: Each CTE does one thing (filter, aggregate, join, compute); split when more than one GROUP BY or JOIN inside
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: CTE doing filter+agg+join = too much. Split into 3 CTEs. Lena's review standard: > 1 GROUP BY or > 1 JOIN per CTE = split required.

questions.question: What must every recursive CTE have to avoid running forever?
options: Termination condition — WHERE clause limiting depth (level < 10) or natural recursion termination
Apache certification — Apache standards require certification for recursive CTEs in production always
Explicit LIMIT — Apache PostgreSQL caps recursive CTEs at 1000 iterations per query unconditionally
Lithuanian regulator approval — every recursive CTE must be approved before production deployment
questions.correctAnswer: Termination condition — WHERE clause limiting depth (level < 10) or natural recursion termination
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: no termination = forever or hits depth limit. WHERE level < 10 or natural termination (each row references parent, roots have no parent).

questions.question: What does Module 8 add to subquery skills?
options: Window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, running aggregates) replace many correlated subqueries / self-joins
More subquery types — Apache standards added 7 new subquery types in 2024 that Module 8 introduces
Materialized subqueries — automatic caching of subquery results across query executions for performance
Async subqueries — running subqueries in parallel for the analytical workload at scale across the cluster
questions.correctAnswer: Window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, running aggregates) replace many correlated subqueries / self-joins
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: window functions = aggregates without collapsing rows. Solve correlated subquery + self-join problems more efficiently and readably.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between GROUP BY and window functions?
options: GROUP BY collapses rows to one per group; window functions preserve rows and attach aggregate to each
GROUP BY uses GROUP BY clause; window functions use OVER clause but produce identical output structure
GROUP BY runs faster than window functions — Apache PostgreSQL optimizer prefers GROUP BY for aggregates
GROUP BY is for aggregation; window functions are only for ranking — they cannot compute SUM or AVG
questions.correctAnswer: GROUP BY collapses rows to one per group; window functions preserve rows and attach aggregate to each
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: GROUP BY = few rows with aggregates. Window = same row count as input + aggregate as additional column. Same input/output, different shape.

questions.question: What does empty `OVER ()` mean?
options: Compute aggregate across ALL rows in the result — no partitioning, no ordering, single window for everything
Compute aggregate across the current row only — Apache standards default to single-row windows always
Compute aggregate across the row group — Apache PostgreSQL infers grouping from the FROM clause always
Compute aggregate across the most recent 100 rows — Apache standards default to a 100-row window size
questions.correctAnswer: Compute aggregate across ALL rows in the result — no partitioning, no ordering, single window for everything
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: OVER () = entire result set as one window. SUM, AVG, COUNT compute across all rows; attach to every output row identically.

questions.question: How do PARTITION BY groups compare to GROUP BY groups?
options: Identical grouping logic — PARTITION BY creates same groups GROUP BY would; difference is row-level vs collapsed output
PARTITION BY is more granular — Apache standards say PARTITION creates 2× the groups of GROUP BY always
PARTITION BY ignores NULLs — Apache PostgreSQL excludes NULL groups from PARTITION BY automatically
PARTITION BY requires ORDER BY — Apache standards forbid unordered partitions in production queries
questions.correctAnswer: Identical grouping logic — PARTITION BY creates same groups GROUP BY would; difference is row-level vs collapsed output
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: PARTITION BY region = same 4 groups GROUP BY region creates. Difference: rows preserved with aggregate attached vs collapsed to one summary row.

questions.question: What changes when you add ORDER BY inside OVER?
options: Window changes from 'entire partition' to 'running window' (each row + all previous in partition) by default
Output ordering changes — ORDER BY inside OVER affects only the result's display order, not values
Apache PostgreSQL caches the window — ORDER BY inside OVER triggers a query plan optimization cache
Lithuanian regulators audit the ORDER BY — every ORDER BY inside OVER is logged for compliance review
questions.correctAnswer: Window changes from 'entire partition' to 'running window' (each row + all previous in partition) by default
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: SUM OVER (PARTITION BY region) = same total per row. + ORDER BY date = running total growing per date. Adding ORDER BY changes what is computed.

questions.question: What is the difference between ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK?
options: ROW_NUMBER: 1,2,3,4 (no ties). RANK: 1,1,3,4 (gaps after ties). DENSE_RANK: 1,1,2,3 (no gaps).
Three identical functions with different names — Apache standards include all three for compatibility only
ROW_NUMBER and RANK identical; DENSE_RANK runs in parallel — Apache PostgreSQL optimization difference
RANK is fastest, DENSE_RANK is slowest, ROW_NUMBER in middle — Apache PostgreSQL benchmark difference
questions.correctAnswer: ROW_NUMBER: 1,2,3,4 (no ties). RANK: 1,1,3,4 (gaps after ties). DENSE_RANK: 1,1,2,3 (no gaps).
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 100,100,90,80 → ROW_NUMBER 1,2,3,4 (arbitrary tie). RANK 1,1,3,4 (gap). DENSE_RANK 1,1,2,3 (no gap). Use ROW_NUMBER for dedup.

questions.question: Why is LAG/LEAD usually preferred over self-joins for day-over-day comparisons?
options: Single-pass over ordered partition vs full self-join — shorter, more readable, typically faster
Apache standards mandate LAG/LEAD over self-joins in production queries — self-joins are deprecated
Self-joins are 10× slower — Apache PostgreSQL benchmark says LAG always beats self-join for comparisons
Lithuanian regulators forbid self-joins — LAG/LEAD is the only approved comparison technique for facts
questions.correctAnswer: Single-pass over ordered partition vs full self-join — shorter, more readable, typically faster
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: M6 lesson 6 self-join (today + yesterday on date+1) = same logic as one LAG expression. Window version = shorter, readable, single-pass faster.

questions.question: What does `ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW` produce?
options: 7-day moving average — current row plus 6 preceding rows in the ordered partition window
Last 6 rows only — Apache standards exclude the current row from BETWEEN frames automatically always
Random 7-row sample — Apache PostgreSQL randomizes rows for statistical sampling within the window
Per-partition reset — every row resets to a new 7-row window starting from itself going backward
questions.correctAnswer: 7-day moving average — current row plus 6 preceding rows in the ordered partition window
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 7-day moving avg = current + 6 preceding rows. Smooths daily fluctuations. Standard in energy analytics, finance, time-series domains.

questions.question: Why does LAST_VALUE with the default frame return the current row's value?
options: Default frame ends at CURRENT ROW — must use ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
Apache PostgreSQL bug — LAST_VALUE was broken in 2023 and Apache standards now require explicit framing
LAST_VALUE is alphabetical — sorts string values and returns the last one in lexicographic order
LAST_VALUE deprecated — Apache standards removed it in 2024 in favor of MAX_VALUE for clarity
questions.correctAnswer: Default frame ends at CURRENT ROW — must use ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: default frame UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW. LAST_VALUE in current frame = current row. Add ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED ... UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING.

questions.question: What pattern uses ROW_NUMBER OVER (PARTITION BY key ORDER BY tiebreaker DESC)?
options: Top-N (especially N=1) deduplication — keep one row per key based on tiebreaker (usually date/timestamp)
Random sampling — Apache PostgreSQL uses ROW_NUMBER for stratified sampling per partition strategy
Compression — Apache standards compress data using ROW_NUMBER to identify duplicate row content
Lineage tracking — every ROW_NUMBER becomes a lineage event in the audit log per Apache compliance
questions.correctAnswer: Top-N (especially N=1) deduplication — keep one row per key based on tiebreaker (usually date/timestamp)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: rn=1 keeps one row per key. Standard SQL deduplication: WHERE rn = 1. HydroAlpes uses this to dedup Bronze where same reading was ingested twice.

questions.question: What does Module 9 build on Module 8's analytical skills?
options: DDL/DML — CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, ALTER, views — building the tables that queries operate against
More window functions — Apache added 12 new window functions in 2024 that Module 9 covers in detail
Performance tuning — query plans, indexes, transactions for the window function workloads at scale
Recursive window functions — combining recursion with windows for hierarchical analytical queries always
questions.correctAnswer: DDL/DML — CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, ALTER, views — building the tables that queries operate against
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: M1-M8 = querying. M9 = creating + modifying tables. CREATE/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/ALTER + views. The other side of SQL.

questions.question: What does `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` check?
options: Only the table name — does not verify existing table has same columns/types/constraints as the statement
Full schema match — Apache standards require IF NOT EXISTS to compare every column and constraint
Encryption status — Apache PostgreSQL refuses IF NOT EXISTS without TLS-encrypted connections always
Replication status — Apache standards require all replicas to confirm before IF NOT EXISTS proceeds
questions.correctAnswer: Only the table name — does not verify existing table has same columns/types/constraints as the statement
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: name-only check. Existing table with different columns = silent success, mismatch undetected. Lena adds separate schema validation after CREATE.

questions.question: What is the database's first validation check on inserted data?
options: Data type — text in DOUBLE PRECISION rejected, length > VARCHAR(20) rejected, invalid date rejected
Index check — Apache PostgreSQL validates against every index before any insert is permitted always
Foreign key — Apache standards check FKs before any other validation including primary key uniqueness
Audit log — Apache PostgreSQL writes audit entry before any insert validation begins automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Data type — text in DOUBLE PRECISION rejected, length > VARCHAR(20) rejected, invalid date rejected
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: type = first validation. Catches issues at insertion before they propagate. '2025-02-30' rejected. Apple in DOUBLE PRECISION rejected.

questions.question: What is HydroAlpes' philosophy on validation in pipelines vs constraints?
options: Both — pipeline validates before insert (clear errors + quarantine); constraints catch what pipeline missed
Pipeline only — Apache standards forbid constraints because they slow inserts in high-volume pipelines
Constraints only — Apache standards mandate all validation in DDL; pipeline code must not validate ever
Neither — Apache PostgreSQL's type system handles all validation through its strict-mode validator only
questions.correctAnswer: Both — pipeline validates before insert (clear errors + quarantine); constraints catch what pipeline missed
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: pipeline = clear errors + quarantine bad data. Constraints = safety net for what pipeline missed. Constraint should never trigger in normal operation.

questions.question: What is the standard Silver-to-Gold transformation pattern?
options: INSERT INTO gold.table SELECT ... FROM silver.table WHERE ... GROUP BY ... — clear, compute, insert
INSERT INTO gold.table VALUES (...) — Apache standards require explicit values for Gold layer always
MERGE INTO gold.table USING ... — Apache PostgreSQL prefers MERGE over INSERT for any production
COPY gold.table FROM 'file.csv' — Apache standards require CSV imports for Gold layer pipelines
questions.correctAnswer: INSERT INTO gold.table SELECT ... FROM silver.table WHERE ... GROUP BY ... — clear, compute, insert
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: INSERT INTO ... SELECT = transformation logic + target direction. Combined with DELETE/TRUNCATE = three-step incremental pipeline foundation.

questions.question: What is the safest pattern for UPDATE at HydroAlpes?
options: STEP 1: SELECT with WHERE to verify rows. STEP 2: UPDATE with same WHERE only after confirmation
STEP 1: BEGIN TRANSACTION. STEP 2: UPDATE. STEP 3: ROLLBACK if wrong. Always BEGIN first.
STEP 1: Backup the table. STEP 2: UPDATE. STEP 3: Restore if wrong. Always backup first.
STEP 1: Apache standards require any UPDATE to go through a code review before execution always
questions.correctAnswer: STEP 1: SELECT with WHERE to verify rows. STEP 2: UPDATE with same WHERE only after confirmation
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: UPDATE cannot be undone without backup restore. Test WHERE with SELECT first. Verify count. Only then UPDATE with same WHERE.

questions.question: What is the rule about UPDATE/DELETE without WHERE clauses?
options: Affects every row in the table — almost certainly not intended; verify with SELECT first to check the count
Apache standards forbid them — PostgreSQL refuses UPDATE/DELETE without WHERE in production always
Lithuanian regulators audit them — every WHERE-less UPDATE/DELETE is flagged for compliance review
Trigger Apache Airflow alerts — every WHERE-less UPDATE/DELETE pages the on-call engineer immediately
questions.correctAnswer: Affects every row in the table — almost certainly not intended; verify with SELECT first to check the count
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: 128M rows updated when expected 12K = something wrong. Always test WHERE with SELECT COUNT(*). Wrap in transaction when possible.

questions.question: What ALTER TABLE operations are safe vs require coordination?
options: Adding nullable column = always safe. Dropping/renaming/narrowing/adding NOT NULL = breaking change requiring downstream coord
All ALTER TABLE = safe — Apache PostgreSQL transactional DDL prevents any downstream impact always
All ALTER TABLE = breaking — Apache standards classify all DDL as breaking changes regardless of type
Apache standards require approval — every ALTER TABLE in production requires Apache certification first
questions.correctAnswer: Adding nullable column = always safe. Dropping/renaming/narrowing/adding NOT NULL = breaking change requiring downstream coord
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 'Add freely, change carefully, drop only after confirming nothing references it.' Applies to every data platform, not just HydroAlpes.

questions.question: What does CTAS (`CREATE TABLE AS SELECT`) materialize?
options: Query result into a physical table — foundation for dbt materializations and Gold-layer tables at HydroAlpes
Database statistics — CTAS computes table statistics for the query optimizer's plan generation always
Index structure — Apache standards say CTAS auto-creates indexes on every column in the target table
Replication slot — CTAS sets up cross-region replication for the resulting table per Apache config
questions.correctAnswer: Query result into a physical table — foundation for dbt materializations and Gold-layer tables at HydroAlpes
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: SELECT defines logic. CTAS stores result. Downstream consumers query stored result instead of recomputing. Foundation of scalable pipeline design.

questions.question: What is the difference between a view and a CTAS table?
options: Table = stored data (fast reads, stale until refreshed). View = saved query (always-fresh, runs on every query)
Table = SQL only. View = Apache standards require views to be in PL/pgSQL not raw SQL globally
Table = readable. View = encrypted by default per Apache PostgreSQL standards for any production
Table = transactional. View = non-transactional per Apache standards for all SELECT-only views always
questions.correctAnswer: Table = stored data (fast reads, stale until refreshed). View = saved query (always-fresh, runs on every query)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: view = stored query, runs every time, always-fresh. CTAS = stored data, fast reads, stale. Views = interface between data engineers and consumers.

questions.question: What does Module 10 close with?
options: SQL inside Spark/dbt/cloud warehouses + query plans/indexes/transactions + complete picture of SQL in DE stack
More DDL — Apache standards added 7 new DDL statements in 2024 that Module 10 introduces in production
PostgreSQL administration — installation, replication, backups, monitoring for Lithuanian production deployments
Window functions deep-dive — Module 10 revisits Module 8 with advanced frame and partition patterns globally
questions.correctAnswer: SQL inside Spark/dbt/cloud warehouses + query plans/indexes/transactions + complete picture of SQL in DE stack
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: M10 = SQL outside its own engine. Spark SQL, dbt, cloud warehouses, data quality, exploration. Concept-level query plans/indexes/transactions.

questions.question: Why is learning PostgreSQL SQL valuable when transitioning to Spark SQL?
options: Analytical thinking transfers — set-based reasoning, aggregation patterns, joins, windows are identical; only execution environment changes
Apache certification carries — PostgreSQL certification automatically applies to Spark SQL deployments always
Performance is identical — Apache PostgreSQL and Spark SQL benchmarks show 0% difference at production
Syntax is identical — Apache standards mandate identical SQL syntax across PostgreSQL and Spark globally
questions.correctAnswer: Analytical thinking transfers — set-based reasoning, aggregation patterns, joins, windows are identical; only execution environment changes
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: same set-based reasoning, aggregation patterns, join strategies, window function design. Foundation makes Spark SQL immediately productive.

questions.question: What is the difference between Spark and cloud warehouses for SQL?
options: Spark has Python AND SQL APIs; cloud warehouses are SQL-first (often only) — primary interface for analysts/BI
Cloud warehouses use proprietary SQL only — Apache standards mandate vendor-specific extensions always
Spark SQL is faster than cloud warehouses by 10× per Apache benchmarks across all data set sizes
Cloud warehouses are deprecated — Apache standards moved to Spark-only SQL execution since 2024 always
questions.correctAnswer: Spark has Python AND SQL APIs; cloud warehouses are SQL-first (often only) — primary interface for analysts/BI
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Spark = Python + SQL APIs. Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift = SQL-first/often-only. Investing in SQL = fluency on every cloud warehouse, present + future.

questions.question: What is the dbt model file's structure?
options: ONLY a SELECT statement — dbt wraps it in CREATE TABLE AS SELECT automatically; CTEs are recommended style
CREATE TABLE statement — dbt requires full DDL in every model file per Apache standards always
Python code with SQL strings — dbt is built around Python orchestration with SQL as embedded queries
JSON configuration — dbt models are pure JSON descriptions; no SQL needed in modern dbt versions
questions.correctAnswer: ONLY a SELECT statement — dbt wraps it in CREATE TABLE AS SELECT automatically; CTEs are recommended style
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: dbt model = SELECT only. dbt wraps in CTAS. Recommended style = CTE chains (Module 7 lesson 8 pattern). Built around CTEs for readability.

questions.question: What is the relationship between SQL skills and data quality checks?
options: SQL itself is the data quality tool — completeness/duplicates/orphans/range checks all use Module 4-6 techniques
Apache certification — only Apache-certified SQL dialects support data quality checks in production globally
Specialized DQ language — data quality requires a separate Apache-standardized check syntax not in core SQL
Lithuanian regulators audit DQ — every data quality check must be approved by regulators before deployment
questions.correctAnswer: SQL itself is the data quality tool — completeness/duplicates/orphans/range checks all use Module 4-6 techniques
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: COUNT (M5), GROUP BY + HAVING (M5), LEFT JOIN + IS NULL (M6), WHERE compound (M4). DQ = same SQL skills. No specialized language.

questions.question: What is the standard exploration query for any new table?
options: SELECT * LIMIT 10 — quick view of structure, types, NULLs, shape without loading millions of rows
SELECT COUNT(*) — Apache standards require row count first before any other exploration always
DESCRIBE table — Apache PostgreSQL standard for inspecting structure programmatically before reads
SHOW INDEXES table — Apache standards require index review before querying for the first time always
questions.correctAnswer: SELECT * LIMIT 10 — quick view of structure, types, NULLs, shape without loading millions of rows
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: every new table → SELECT * LIMIT 10. Structure + types + samples + NULL presence + general shape. Same as M3 lesson 7.

questions.question: What does a query plan show?
options: How the optimizer chose to execute the query — table scans vs index usage, join algorithms, sorting steps
How long the query took to run — Apache standards include execution time as the only meaningful plan info
How many rows the query returned — Apache PostgreSQL plan output is purely a result count summary
How the query was encrypted — Apache standards encrypt every query plan for compliance globally always
questions.correctAnswer: How the optimizer chose to execute the query — table scans vs index usage, join algorithms, sorting steps
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: EXPLAIN reveals optimizer's chosen plan. Concept-level introduction in M10 — full performance tuning is the senior track topic.

questions.question: What does an index do for queries?
options: Speeds lookups on filtered/joined columns — but adds overhead to writes (every insert updates the index)
Encrypts the data — Apache PostgreSQL indexes are encrypted by default per security standards always
Replicates the data — Apache standards say indexes are local copies of the column for fast access
Compresses the data — Apache PostgreSQL indexes are compressed Parquet copies of indexed columns
questions.correctAnswer: Speeds lookups on filtered/joined columns — but adds overhead to writes (every insert updates the index)
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: indexes speed reads, slow writes. Concept-level intro in M10. Trade-offs and tuning are advanced topics covered in later tracks.

questions.question: What is the value of transactions in SQL?
options: All-or-nothing — multiple statements succeed together or none apply, ensuring data consistency
Apache standards require them — every SQL statement must be in a transaction in production always
Performance — Apache PostgreSQL transactions run 10× faster than non-transactional execution always
Encryption — Apache standards encrypt every transaction's data automatically for compliance globally
questions.correctAnswer: All-or-nothing — multiple statements succeed together or none apply, ensuring data consistency
