questions.correctAnswer: Spark fetches every row across the wire and filters locally — silently slow
questions.explanation: Failed pushdown is silent: results are correct but Spark transfers the whole table; CI checks should assert critical filters appear in PushedFilters.

questions.question: Partition column selection at AlpineEnergy starts with:
options: A consumer survey ranking columns by weighted query frequency across pipelines
A coin flip between the two engineers most familiar with the table at hand
Always picking the primary key column because it is the most unique by definition
Picking the most recently added column to spread data evenly across new files
questions.correctAnswer: A consumer survey ranking columns by weighted query frequency across pipelines
questions.explanation: Multi-consumer partition design is data-driven: enumerate consumers, score columns by weighted query frequency, choose what serves the aggregate best.

questions.question: PostgreSQL with 1,100 daily partitions vs 36 monthly partitions usually:
options: Pays meaningfully higher planner overhead per query — daily is hard to justify
Performs identically because the planner caches partition metadata across queries
Runs faster because more partitions enable more parallel sequential scans always
Cannot be created at all — PostgreSQL caps partitions at 100 per parent table
questions.correctAnswer: Pays meaningfully higher planner overhead per query — daily is hard to justify
questions.explanation: The planner evaluates partition constraints per query; 1,100 partitions add 50-200 ms planning overhead — monthly is the practical sweet spot for PG.

questions.question: Range partitioning on meter_id (string IDs A-E, F-J, ...) on highly skewed data leads to:
options: Skewed partitions where dominant ID prefixes get disproportionately more rows
Uniformly distributed partitions because B-tree indexes balance themselves
Improved compression ratios compared with date-based range partitioning today
Automatic re-balancing by PostgreSQL when partition sizes diverge over time
questions.correctAnswer: Skewed partitions where dominant ID prefixes get disproportionately more rows
questions.explanation: Range on a skewed column produces hot partitions; for parallelism without pruning, hash partitioning is the right tool, not range on skewed keys.

questions.question: Hash partitioning is the right choice when the goal is:
options: Uniform parallelism without dependency on a range or list filter predicate
Pruning by date filters using the standard date >= and date < range operators
Pruning by country code with explicit per-country physical isolation requirements
Producing maximum compression ratios on string columns inside Parquet files
questions.correctAnswer: Uniform parallelism without dependency on a range or list filter predicate
questions.explanation: Hash distributes rows uniformly via key hashing — good for balanced parallel scans, but cannot support range/list pruning.

questions.question: A WHERE date = '2025-01-15'::TIMESTAMP filter on a DATE-partitioned table can:
options: Lose partition pruning entirely due to the column-level type mismatch involved
Partition-prune normally because PostgreSQL casts implicitly during planning
Run faster than the canonical DATE form because TIMESTAMP indexes are denser
Cause the optimizer to recreate the partition layout dynamically on every run
questions.correctAnswer: Lose partition pruning entirely due to the column-level type mismatch involved
questions.explanation: Type mismatch between filter and partition key can defeat constraint exclusion; ensure the filter type exactly matches the partition column type.

questions.question: Sub-partitioning depth at AlpineEnergy is capped at 2 levels for PostgreSQL because:
options: Planner overhead and VACUUM time grow rapidly with leaf partition count
PostgreSQL's SQL parser refuses to compile any third level of partitioning syntax
Three-level partitioning would require a full database restart on every query
CHECK constraints on partition boundaries cannot be combined past two levels
questions.correctAnswer: Planner overhead and VACUUM time grow rapidly with leaf partition count
questions.explanation: Each leaf adds catalog entries, planner work, and maintenance cost; PG's practical limit is ~200 leaves — Delta Lake tolerates much deeper schemes.

questions.question: Live-table repartitioning at terabyte scale via the dual-write pattern typically takes:
options: 8-12 weeks: setup, backfill, dual-write window, consumer migration, decommission
A few hours of downtime during the final overnight maintenance window only
Less than one day because PostgreSQL provides an instant repartition operator
Forever — terabyte tables can never be repartitioned once they are in production
questions.correctAnswer: 8-12 weeks: setup, backfill, dual-write window, consumer migration, decommission
questions.explanation: Repartitioning a live TB+ table is a multi-week project: build new table, automated backfill, dual-write, gradual consumer migration, then decommission.

questions.question: Sharding becomes necessary (vs partitioning) when:
options: A single instance cannot handle write throughput, concurrency, or data residency
The table exceeds 100 GB regardless of any other workload characteristics
The DBA prefers a distributed system because it sounds more impressive on a CV
The query plan contains more than five join nodes including subquery scans
questions.correctAnswer: A single instance cannot handle write throughput, concurrency, or data residency
questions.explanation: Single-instance partitioning serves most workloads; shard only when one instance is genuinely the bottleneck or residency requires it.

questions.question: A partition existence check that runs daily at 02:00 catches:
options: Automation failures — missing partitions before the pipeline's first INSERT fails
Schema drift introduced by manual ALTER TABLE statements during business hours
Late-arriving CDC events that cannot find a target partition for their timestamp
Replication lag between primary and standby that exceeds the configured threshold
questions.correctAnswer: Automation failures — missing partitions before the pipeline's first INSERT fails
questions.explanation: A 3-month lookahead existence check finds the gap before the pipeline tries to insert data, giving on-call time to create the missing partition.

questions.question: A leftover September 2022 partition discovered after a retention failure indicates:
options: A retention automation bug — likely a timezone or date arithmetic edge case
A successful new feature that intentionally extends data retention by one month
An expected behaviour because PostgreSQL never drops partitions automatically
A storage cost saving because old partitions cost less per byte than new ones
questions.correctAnswer: A retention automation bug — likely a timezone or date arithmetic edge case
questions.explanation: A partition older than the retention window is a retention check failure; the catch is what monitoring is for — fix the date logic, drop the leftover.

questions.question: Classifying a multi-table pipeline as a "star topology" implies:
options: One large fact table joined with many small dimensions — broadcast all dimensions
A linear chain of joins where each output feeds the next stage in sequence
A cross-engine federation that cannot benefit from any local optimization
A self-join where the table is joined to itself across multiple recursion levels
questions.correctAnswer: One large fact table joined with many small dimensions — broadcast all dimensions
questions.explanation: Star = fact + N small dimensions; the standard optimization is broadcasting dimensions and letting the CBO reorder dimension joins freely.

questions.question: Replicating a small slowly-changing dimension nightly to Delta Lake instead of federating it via JDBC:
options: Trades freshness for full Catalyst optimization — broadcast, pushdown, AQE all work
Always produces incorrect joins because nightly replication loses referential integrity
Eliminates the dimension entirely so consumers must use placeholder defaults forever
Forces every query to read from PostgreSQL because Delta cannot store dimensions
questions.correctAnswer: Trades freshness for full Catalyst optimization — broadcast, pushdown, AQE all work
questions.explanation: A local Delta-to-Delta join gets full Catalyst optimisation; the cost is dimension staleness — acceptable for things that change at most daily.

questions.question: Pre-resolving a temporal join at load time means:
options: Storing the dimension surrogate key in the fact, turning later joins into equality
Re-running the temporal join on every consumer query for maximum freshness
Caching the dimension in memory and rejecting queries on cold dimension entries
Materialising the temporal range as a 2-D array column inside the fact table
questions.correctAnswer: Storing the dimension surrogate key in the fact, turning later joins into equality
questions.explanation: The Silver-to-Gold load resolves each fact to its correct dimension version once; downstream consumers do hash equality joins on the surrogate key.

questions.question: PostgreSQL join elimination from a wide view typically requires:
options: INNER joins, no columns selected from the joined table, and FK + NOT NULL constraints
Materialised views with WITH NO DATA for every joinable dimension table involved
Manual hints injected by pg_hint_plan for every query that uses the wide view
A query that explicitly says ELIMINATE in its hint comment for that specific join
questions.correctAnswer: INNER joins, no columns selected from the joined table, and FK + NOT NULL constraints
questions.explanation: Elimination needs proof that the join cannot filter rows or contribute columns — INNER + unused side + FK + NOT NULL gives the planner that proof.

questions.question: Materialised enrichment views save platform compute when:
options: Three or more consumers execute the same multi-table join independently
The join executes only once per quarter for a low-volume regulatory report
Each consumer requires a different freshness guarantee from the same data
The dimensions change every minute and consumers need the absolute latest value
questions.correctAnswer: Three or more consumers execute the same multi-table join independently
questions.explanation: Pre-computing a join consumed by N pipelines replaces N executions with 1 refresh; high-fanout joins are the prime targets.

questions.question: Adding a hash-bucket column to a join key (e.g. join_bucket = abs(hashtext(id)) % 16) addresses:
options: Skew on a heavy join value by spreading it across multiple uniform partitions
Storage cost by removing the original join column from the indexed columns list
Authentication issues by hashing user identifiers before the join is executed
Plan stability by forcing the optimizer to always pick the same join algorithm
questions.correctAnswer: Skew on a heavy join value by spreading it across multiple uniform partitions
questions.explanation: Composite key (key, hash_bucket) splits a hot key across N buckets, giving balanced partition sizes — an architectural fix for chronic skew.

questions.question: Adding a materialization boundary in a 6-table chain pipeline helps because:
options: Two 3-table joins are within the DP planner's capacity, so each is optimal
Materialisation adds randomness to the query plan, improving robustness over time
Spark requires that every CTE be materialised to disk before any join can begin
The optimizer cannot read intermediate CTE results without an explicit boundary
questions.correctAnswer: Two 3-table joins are within the DP planner's capacity, so each is optimal
questions.explanation: Splitting a 6-table chain into 3+3 keeps each sub-query within DP's sweet spot and the temp-table's ANALYZE gives the second stage accurate stats.

questions.question: Bloom filter pushdown in Spark SQL is most effective when:
options: The join key has low cardinality so the filter is small with low false positive rate
The fact table is unpartitioned so the filter must scan every file regardless
The dimension side's build is too large to broadcast across the executors
The query is wrapped in a CTE that disables Catalyst's pushdown rule entirely
questions.correctAnswer: The join key has low cardinality so the filter is small with low false positive rate
questions.explanation: Low-cardinality keys produce small Bloom filters with low false-positive rates; high-cardinality keys grow the filter and reduce its skipping benefit.

questions.question: PostgreSQL's parallel hash join spills to disk when:
options: The shared hash table does not fit in work_mem times the number of workers
The input table contains a column with a domain-specific user-defined type
The query references more than 100 rows in the parallel scan output set
Two workers attempt to hash the same row at the same time during the build phase
questions.correctAnswer: The shared hash table does not fit in work_mem times the number of workers
questions.explanation: When the shared build exceeds the per-query memory budget, PG partitions to disk (Batches > 1) and re-probes — typically 5-10x slower than in-memory.

questions.question: The most effective optimization for a chronic 30-minute join in a TB+ pipeline is often:
options: Eliminating the join via a materialised view, pre-aggregation, or partition alignment
Increasing work_mem repeatedly until the join eventually fits in available RAM
Adding more executor cores until the wall-clock execution time falls below 10 minutes
Disabling AQE so that the join uses the original compile-time chosen physical plan
questions.correctAnswer: Eliminating the join via a materialised view, pre-aggregation, or partition alignment
questions.explanation: The cheapest 30-minute join is the one that does not run at all — materialise, pre-aggregate, or co-partition before tuning the join algorithm itself.

questions.question: Informal schema governance fails at 10-team scale because:
options: Notification, commitment, and visibility asymmetries cause coordinated breaks
Slack technically refuses to deliver messages to channels with too many members
PostgreSQL automatically rejects any schema change after the third announcement
CI systems become incapable of running tests once team count exceeds five teams
questions.correctAnswer: Notification, commitment, and visibility asymmetries cause coordinated breaks
questions.explanation: At enterprise scale Slack-based deprecation breaks: producer doesn't know all consumers, ack != migration, and hidden notebook consumers exist.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy chose a Git-based YAML schema registry because:
options: It provides versioning, code review, and CI validation without a new service
Confluent Schema Registry refused to integrate with their PostgreSQL CDC pipeline
YAML files are required by the EU Data Governance Act for relational systems
Git is the only system that PostgreSQL trusts as a source of schema declarations
questions.correctAnswer: It provides versioning, code review, and CI validation without a new service
questions.explanation: Git provides 90% of the registry value (versioning, PR review, CI validation) with near-zero operational overhead vs a dedicated registry service.

questions.question: A data contract differs from the registry because the contract:
options: Declares which columns and SLAs each specific consumer depends on for its work
Records every column that has ever existed in the producer's tables historically
Forces the producer team to grant SELECT to every team in the organization
Replaces the schema registry once it has been signed by both teams involved
questions.correctAnswer: Declares which columns and SLAs each specific consumer depends on for its work
questions.explanation: Registry = what the table looks like; contract = what the consumer depends on. The contract makes producer-consumer dependency machine-readable.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy's automated impact analysis on a column rename produces:
options: A list of every contract that depends on the column with team and SLA tier
A patch file that automatically rewrites every consumer query to the new name
A direct execution of the rename against production without further review steps
A backup of the table's data that can be used for a rollback within one hour
questions.correctAnswer: A list of every contract that depends on the column with team and SLA tier
questions.explanation: The CI hook queries every contract referencing the changed column and reports affected teams, pipelines, and tier — input to the SCR negotiation.

questions.question: Producer-side contract tests verify:
options: The actual schema (information_schema) matches the declared schema (registry)
Every consumer pipeline still produces the same numerical totals after a deploy
The producer team has the highest permission level on the production database
No row in the table has been modified since the last successful CI build run
questions.correctAnswer: The actual schema (information_schema) matches the declared schema (registry)
questions.explanation: Producer-side tests catch schema drift: the migration must produce a schema matching the registry; runs in CI on a fresh DB after migrations apply.

questions.question: Consumer-side contract tests live in:
options: The consuming team's CI pipeline — testing their query against the declared schema
The producing team's repository so the producer can refactor on the consumer's behalf
A central database that all teams must connect to before every commit they make
The schema registry YAML file as inline SQL embedded in the contract document itself
questions.correctAnswer: The consuming team's CI pipeline — testing their query against the declared schema
questions.explanation: Consumer-side tests are owned and maintained by the consumer; the producer's deploy pipeline runs them via cross-repo CI integration.

questions.question: Adding a NULLABLE column to a Silver table is a backward-compatible change because:
options: Existing consumer queries do not reference the new column and continue to work
The PostgreSQL planner automatically rewrites queries to include the new column
NULLs are stored more efficiently than non-null values in PostgreSQL's heap
The new column inherits the GRANT privileges of the most-privileged existing column
questions.correctAnswer: Existing consumer queries do not reference the new column and continue to work
questions.explanation: Additive nullable changes do not alter existing query semantics; the fast path through governance reflects this lower risk.

questions.question: A NUMERIC(18,4) value passed through a Spark SQL division and written back to PostgreSQL can:
options: Lose precision due to DecimalType rounding behaviour at the engine boundary
Be silently coerced into INTEGER, dropping every fractional digit completely
Be promoted to DOUBLE and pollute the audit log with floating-point representations
Trigger an automatic schema migration that widens the column to NUMERIC(38,8)
questions.correctAnswer: Lose precision due to DecimalType rounding behaviour at the engine boundary
questions.explanation: Cross-engine NUMERIC <-> Decimal can shift the last digit on division. Reconciliation tests across engines catch the cumulative billing impact.

questions.question: Column-level lineage answers questions that table-level lineage cannot, such as:
options: Which downstream pipelines actually reference the specific column being changed
How many rows the table contains at the moment the schema change is proposed
Whether the encryption key for the column has been rotated within the quarter
Which database role last queried the table during the prior 24 hour window
questions.correctAnswer: Which downstream pipelines actually reference the specific column being changed
questions.explanation: Table-level lineage tells you which pipelines read the table; column-level lineage tells you which of them touch the specific column being changed.

questions.question: In the schema-governance maturity model, contracts (Stage 3) are typically adopted when:
options: Cross-engine flows begin or cross-team breaking changes exceed roughly 2 per quarter
The very first table is created in any new database before any teams onboard
A regulator imposes a fine on the platform team for an unrelated security incident
PostgreSQL reaches its connection limit on a single shared instance during peak hours
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-engine flows begin or cross-team breaking changes exceed roughly 2 per quarter
questions.explanation: Contracts add governance overhead; introduce them when the failure rate or cross-team coordination cost justifies the formalism.

questions.question: PostgreSQL's process-per-connection model contributes ~5-10 MB private memory per backend, so:
options: A connection pooler is essential at enterprise scale to bound memory usage
PostgreSQL becomes faster than thread-based databases above 1,000 connections
Each backend automatically shares its sort buffer with all other connected backends
Connection pooling is harmful because it prevents PostgreSQL from forking processes
questions.correctAnswer: A connection pooler is essential at enterprise scale to bound memory usage
questions.explanation: Backend memory linearly grows with connection count; PgBouncer multiplexes many logical connections onto a smaller backend pool.

questions.question: In PostgreSQL MVCC, an UPDATE creates:
options: A new tuple version while marking the old version as invalidated by xmax
A direct overwrite of the existing tuple, bypassing the visibility checks
A row in a hidden journal table while leaving the heap pages untouched
An implicit DELETE followed by an INSERT that takes a separate transaction ID
questions.correctAnswer: A new tuple version while marking the old version as invalidated by xmax
questions.explanation: MVCC is non-overwrite: UPDATE writes a new tuple and sets xmax on the old one; the old version is reclaimed only by VACUUM.

questions.question: Transaction ID wraparound risk grows when:
options: VACUUM cannot keep up with freezing — typically due to long-lived blockers
The database is restored from a backup taken more than seven days ago
The connection pool size exceeds max_connections in postgresql.conf settings
A single transaction commits more than 4 billion rows in one statement
questions.correctAnswer: VACUUM cannot keep up with freezing — typically due to long-lived blockers
questions.explanation: PG uses 32-bit XIDs; freezing requires VACUUM. A long "idle in transaction" or autovacuum blocker prevents freezing and pushes age toward wraparound.

questions.question: In pg_stat_bgwriter, a high buffers_backend counter relative to total writes means:
options: Backends are doing I/O that the background writer/checkpointer should handle
The replica is receiving more WAL than the primary is generating per second
Autovacuum has been disabled and is not running on any production tables anymore
TLS encryption is failing on the network layer between client and server processes
questions.correctAnswer: Backends are doing I/O that the background writer/checkpointer should handle
questions.explanation: High buffers_backend means dirty buffer evictions during query execution — usually shared_buffers too small or background writer too slow.

questions.question: Standard VACUUM (lazy) versus VACUUM FULL: lazy VACUUM:
options: Reclaims space within existing pages without shrinking the table file size
Always rewrites the entire table to a new file regardless of dead tuple count
Requires an AccessExclusiveLock identical to the lock taken by VACUUM FULL
Skips index entries entirely so VACUUM does no index maintenance work at all
questions.correctAnswer: Reclaims space within existing pages without shrinking the table file size
questions.explanation: Lazy VACUUM marks dead space reusable but does not return file size; VACUUM FULL rewrites the file but takes an AccessExclusiveLock.

questions.question: The lock mode acquired by INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on a table is:
options: RowExclusiveLock — multiple writers can write to the same table concurrently
AccessExclusiveLock — every concurrent reader and writer is blocked completely
ShareLock — no other writer can modify any row until the transaction commits
AccessShareLock — multiple writers and readers share the same lock identifier
questions.correctAnswer: RowExclusiveLock — multiple writers can write to the same table concurrently
questions.explanation: DML takes RowExclusiveLock at the table level; multiple writers coexist, with row-level conflicts resolved separately by tuple locks.

questions.question: A PostgreSQL HOT update succeeds when:
options: No indexed column is updated AND the new tuple fits on the same page as the old
Both indexed and unindexed columns are updated atomically in one statement
The page has more than 50% free space irrespective of which columns are updated
The table has no indexes at all on any column in any of its currently active rows
questions.correctAnswer: No indexed column is updated AND the new tuple fits on the same page as the old
questions.explanation: HOT keeps the new tuple on the same page and chains via line pointers — no index update — when no indexed column changed and there is room.

questions.question: PgBouncer's transaction pooling mode means:
options: A backend is assigned per transaction and returned to the pool at COMMIT
Each session is permanently bound to one backend for its entire connection lifetime
Each individual SQL statement may be routed to a different backend at random
Backends are pre-allocated at startup and never recycled across applications
questions.correctAnswer: A backend is assigned per transaction and returned to the pool at COMMIT
questions.explanation: Transaction pooling shares backends across many logical connections at transaction granularity — high efficiency, but breaks session-state features.

questions.question: pg_stat_statements is most valuable for:
options: Identifying performance regression candidates and inefficient query patterns
Replacing the optimizer's cost estimates with actual measured wall-clock times
Capturing every transaction's WAL record for replication to a standby instance
Encrypting query text before storing it in the database's audit log table
questions.correctAnswer: Identifying performance regression candidates and inefficient query patterns
questions.explanation: pg_stat_statements records cumulative stats per query pattern: count, time, I/O — the primary tool for "which queries deserve optimisation effort?".

questions.question: A custom PostgreSQL extension written in C that has a buffer overflow can:
options: Crash the backend or corrupt shared memory and bring down the whole cluster
Be automatically isolated by the OS so only the one calling session is affected
Be detected and reverted by PostgreSQL's built-in extension sandboxing layer
Cause only a transaction rollback because PostgreSQL recovers from any crash
questions.correctAnswer: Crash the backend or corrupt shared memory and bring down the whole cluster
questions.explanation: C extensions run inside the backend; memory corruption can crash the process or the cluster — extensions need the same review rigor as core code.

questions.question: PostgreSQL line pointers in a heap page enable HOT updates by:
options: Letting indexes point to a stable line pointer that re-routes to the latest tuple
Compressing tuple data inline with the line pointer for higher density per page
Encrypting each tuple's payload while leaving the line pointer in plaintext
Locking the entire page exclusively during every UPDATE for atomicity guarantees
questions.correctAnswer: Letting indexes point to a stable line pointer that re-routes to the latest tuple
questions.explanation: Indexes reference line pointers, not raw offsets; on HOT update the line pointer redirects to the new in-page tuple and indexes need no update.

questions.question: A row group in Parquet is the unit of:
options: Parallelism — each Spark task reads one row group at a time
Compression — the entire row group is compressed with one codec call
Indexing — Parquet stores B-tree indexes inside each row group footer
Replication — Spark replicates row groups across executors for fault tolerance
questions.correctAnswer: Parallelism — each Spark task reads one row group at a time
questions.explanation: Spark allocates one task per row group; column chunks within a row group are the unit of column pruning.

questions.question: Z-ORDER on (date, region) in Delta Lake improves data skipping because:
options: It co-locates similar values, tightening per-file min/max statistics on those columns
It rewrites the Parquet schema so the columns become true B-tree indexes on disk
It enables PostgreSQL to query Delta files directly without a Spark cluster
It encrypts the row group statistics so attackers cannot infer data distributions
questions.correctAnswer: It co-locates similar values, tightening per-file min/max statistics on those columns
questions.explanation: Z-ORDER multi-dimensionally clusters data; row group statistics get tighter ranges, eliminating files that would otherwise be scanned and discarded.

questions.question: Dictionary encoding in Parquet is most effective for:
options: Low-cardinality string columns where one dictionary fits in each column chunk
Floating-point columns with continuous numeric values across the full table
Time-series timestamps that increase monotonically with no repeated values
BLOB columns containing JPEG image bytes that vary across every single row
questions.correctAnswer: Low-cardinality string columns where one dictionary fits in each column chunk
questions.explanation: Dictionary maps distinct values to small integers; very effective when distinct count per chunk is low (status codes, country, customer_type).

questions.question: Delta Lake's transaction log contains:
options: JSON entries with add/remove file actions plus per-file statistics for skipping
A copy of every row that has ever been inserted into the table, in append order
The Parquet footers of every active file concatenated for fast metadata access
A binary log of every executor stack trace produced during query execution
questions.correctAnswer: JSON entries with add/remove file actions plus per-file statistics for skipping
questions.explanation: The _delta_log/*.json files record file-level add/remove transactions and statistics; readers use them to assemble the current snapshot.

questions.question: Delta Lake's default delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols = 32 means:
options: Only the first 32 columns by schema position have min/max statistics for skipping
Only 32 rows per file participate in the data skipping evaluation each query
A maximum of 32 partitions per table can be created before skipping is disabled
Statistics are computed on 32 random columns chosen each time OPTIMIZE runs
questions.correctAnswer: Only the first 32 columns by schema position have min/max statistics for skipping
questions.explanation: For tables with > 32 columns, columns past position 32 have no stats and no skipping; reorder columns or raise the limit at metadata cost.

questions.question: Object storage (ADLS, S3) throughput scales with:
options: Parallelism — one task gets ~60 MB/s, 100 tasks get ~6 GB/s aggregate throughput
Single-stream speed — adding more tasks does not improve aggregate throughput
CPU clock speed of the executors that issue the GET requests in parallel
PostgreSQL's shared_buffers configuration on the dedicated query coordinator
questions.correctAnswer: Parallelism — one task gets ~60 MB/s, 100 tasks get ~6 GB/s aggregate throughput
questions.explanation: Object stores are throughput-by-parallelism systems: aggregate scales with concurrent readers — Spark's task model fits this perfectly.

questions.question: For a query that selects 2 of 6 columns from 37M rows, columnar storage typically beats row-store by:
options: Reading only the 2 needed column chunks instead of every byte of every tuple
Maintaining a hidden secondary index on each column for faster lookup access
Replicating each column to every node so no network transfer is ever needed
Forcing the query engine to use sort-merge instead of hash-based aggregations
questions.correctAnswer: Reading only the 2 needed column chunks instead of every byte of every tuple
questions.explanation: Column pruning is the primary columnar win for analytics: skip the columns the query doesn't reference; row-store always reads the full tuple.

questions.question: Customer-managed key encryption versus provider-managed key encryption requires:
options: A tested key recovery procedure — losing the key permanently destroys the data
A second PostgreSQL instance dedicated solely to storing the active key material
Disabling all client TLS so the key can be transmitted in plaintext for performance
A monthly schema migration that re-encrypts every row using a new column type
questions.correctAnswer: A tested key recovery procedure — losing the key permanently destroys the data
questions.explanation: CMK gives stronger control but irreversible loss risk; quarterly tested key backups are non-negotiable to avoid catastrophic data loss.

questions.question: A MySQL DATETIME column migrated to PostgreSQL TIMESTAMPTZ without explicit conversion can:
options: Shift every value by the local-time offset because MySQL has no timezone info
Fail to compile because PostgreSQL refuses any value that is not in UTC initially
Improve query performance because TIMESTAMPTZ is faster than DATETIME generally
Remain identical because the SQL standard guarantees timezone interoperability
questions.correctAnswer: Shift every value by the local-time offset because MySQL has no timezone info
questions.explanation: MySQL DATETIME is naive; reading it as UTC into TIMESTAMPTZ when it was actually local time shifts every value by the offset — explicit CONVERT_TZ required.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy's hybrid multi-tenancy model uses:
options: Different isolation mechanisms (instance, schema, RLS) matched to regulatory needs
A single shared schema with no isolation because all teams trust each other fully
A separate PostgreSQL instance for every team in every country at all times
Application-level row filtering applied uniformly to every database query at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Different isolation mechanisms (instance, schema, RLS) matched to regulatory needs
questions.explanation: Match isolation strength to requirement: instance for residency, schema for country access, RLS for team-level — over-isolation wastes resources.

questions.question: PostgreSQL multiple RLS policies combine by default with:
options: AND (restrictive) — every policy must allow the row for it to be visible
OR (permissive) — any policy that allows the row makes it visible to the user
XOR — the row is visible iff exactly one of the active policies allows it
A random choice between AND and OR refreshed on every SET ROLE command
questions.correctAnswer: AND (restrictive) — every policy must allow the row for it to be visible
questions.explanation: Default RLS combination is AND (restrictive): all policies must permit the row. ELSE FALSE provides default-deny inside each policy expression.

questions.question: A masking view that returns full PII to an unintended role is:
options: A data breach — the masking control failed and the test suite must catch it
A normal occurrence handled by post-incident audit logging and notification
A bug that PostgreSQL automatically corrects via internal RLS escalation rules
A scenario that cannot happen because views always inherit base table privileges
questions.correctAnswer: A data breach — the masking control failed and the test suite must catch it
questions.explanation: A view that exposes PII to the wrong role is a breach, not a bug — CI tests should impersonate every role and assert masking levels match.

questions.question: pgaudit's value over PostgreSQL's log_statement = 'all' is that it:
options: Provides fine-grained per-role and per-object audit logging at lower volume
Encrypts every audit row by default using the cluster's server certificate
Replaces the WAL with a complete record of every read access for replication
Works without any configuration because it auto-detects which tables hold PII
questions.correctAnswer: Provides fine-grained per-role and per-object audit logging at lower volume
questions.explanation: pgaudit lets you audit only what compliance requires (specific roles or objects), avoiding the volume blow-up of logging every statement.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy enables cross-country analytics under residency constraints by:
options: Anonymising data before cross-border transfer; raw PII never leaves its country
Compressing the PII column with AES-256 so it appears anonymised on transit
Storing all data in one shared lake and relying on RLS policies for residency
Disabling cross-country queries entirely until each regulator approves them manually
questions.correctAnswer: Anonymising data before cross-border transfer; raw PII never leaves its country
questions.explanation: Residency forbids PII transfer, not analytics. Strip PII at the boundary; the shared lake holds only anonymised columns.

questions.question: Spark SQL pool weight = 3 vs weight = 1 means under contention:
options: The weight-3 pool receives 3x the share of cluster resources of the weight-1 pool
The weight-3 pool always preempts the weight-1 pool entirely until idle again
The weight-3 pool runs three times as many parallel tasks per executor core used
The weight-3 pool can use 3x as much driver memory per query simultaneously
questions.correctAnswer: The weight-3 pool receives 3x the share of cluster resources of the weight-1 pool
questions.explanation: Fair scheduler weights determine relative share during contention; not preemption, not multiplied parallelism — a proportional share of the pie.

questions.question: TLS 1.3 enforcement at AlpineEnergy is implemented through:
options: ssl = on, ssl_min_protocol_version = TLSv1.3, and pg_hba hostnossl reject rules
A single check inside every application that compares the protocol string in code
Application-layer encryption that wraps every SQL statement in a JSON envelope
Disabling all TCP connections in favour of named pipes on the loopback interface
questions.correctAnswer: ssl = on, ssl_min_protocol_version = TLSv1.3, and pg_hba hostnossl reject rules
questions.explanation: TLS at the database is configured in postgresql.conf and enforced via pg_hba.conf; rejecting non-SSL connections is the safety guarantee.

questions.question: A SECURITY DEFINER function is a privilege-escalation risk because:
options: It runs with the creator's privileges, potentially exposing data to weaker callers
It bypasses all PostgreSQL RLS policies that have been applied to base tables
It cannot be revoked once it has been granted to any role in the database
It runs only on the standby server, where security policies are not enforced
questions.correctAnswer: It runs with the creator's privileges, potentially exposing data to weaker callers
questions.explanation: A definer-privileged function lets less-privileged callers run code that reads data they cannot read directly — a classic escalation vector.

questions.question: GDPR's 72-hour notification deadline begins:
options: When the organization becomes aware of a possible breach, not when it is confirmed
When the official forensic report is signed and approved by the legal team
When the supervisory authority sends an explicit confirmation request to the firm
When the post-incident review document is published on the company intranet
questions.correctAnswer: When the organization becomes aware of a possible breach, not when it is confirmed
questions.explanation: Awareness, not confirmation, starts the clock — file a preliminary notification quickly and a detailed one within the window.

questions.question: Audit logs become non-repudiable when:
options: They are stored in a separate instance that no operational role can modify
Every audit row is signed by the user who triggered the audit before storage
They are encrypted with the same key used for the data they describe today
They are deleted after 30 days so attackers have no chance to tamper with them
questions.correctAnswer: They are stored in a separate instance that no operational role can modify
questions.explanation: Separation of duties: the role that can access PII must not be the role that can edit the audit record of that access.

questions.question: A streaming proposal is rejected at AlpineEnergy when:
options: The stated freshness requirement can be met by increasing batch frequency instead
The team has not yet completed the senior-level streaming SQL certification course
The Kafka cluster is more than 80% utilised regardless of the actual business need
Spark Structured Streaming's checkpoint format does not match the team's preferred schema
questions.correctAnswer: The stated freshness requirement can be met by increasing batch frequency instead
questions.explanation: Streaming carries 24/7 operational cost; if a 1-hour batch produces the required freshness, batch wins on simplicity.

questions.question: A PostgreSQL replication slot held by an offline consumer:
options: Continues retaining WAL indefinitely, risking disk exhaustion on the primary
Self-destructs after 1 hour of inactivity, freeing the WAL for reuse instantly
Is automatically reassigned to the next available consumer in the publication
Pauses primary writes until the consumer catches up with all retained WAL
questions.correctAnswer: Continues retaining WAL indefinitely, risking disk exhaustion on the primary
questions.explanation: A non-consuming slot keeps WAL pinned; if the consumer is offline long enough, the disk fills and PG halts — monitoring is critical.

questions.question: Debezium's MilliSecondsBehindSource metric measures:
options: The delay between a change in PostgreSQL and its publication to Kafka
The clock skew between the connector's host and the upstream PostgreSQL primary
The number of milliseconds since the last connector configuration change
The age of the oldest WAL segment retained by the replication slot in flight
questions.correctAnswer: The delay between a change in PostgreSQL and its publication to Kafka
questions.explanation: It's the WAL-to-Kafka end-to-end lag — the primary health signal for the connector, with thresholds at 60s warning and 300s critical.

questions.question: Watermarking in Spark Structured Streaming declares:
options: A threshold beyond which late-arriving events are discarded from open windows
A guaranteed exactly-once semantic across every consumer in the cluster
A maximum trigger interval below which micro-batches will not be processed
A signature appended to each event so consumers can verify its authenticity
questions.correctAnswer: A threshold beyond which late-arriving events are discarded from open windows
questions.explanation: Watermarks let the engine close windows by dropping events older than the watermark — the tradeoff between completeness and bounded state.

questions.question: A 30-second trigger interval running 24 hours generates:
options: ~2,880 micro-batch outputs per day, requiring scheduled OPTIMIZE compaction
A single large file at the end of the day after compaction settles overnight
No additional file overhead because Delta Lake batches micro-batches transparently
Exactly one Parquet file per partition regardless of trigger interval frequency
questions.correctAnswer: ~2,880 micro-batch outputs per day, requiring scheduled OPTIMIZE compaction
questions.explanation: Each micro-batch writes ≥ 1 small file; without scheduled OPTIMIZE the small-file count grows unboundedly and degrades scan planning.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy's practical exactly-once is achieved by:
options: At-least-once delivery with idempotent MERGE sinks keyed on the natural event key
A pure exactly-once pipeline with no duplicate-handling logic anywhere downstream
A real-time consensus algorithm distributed across all participating Spark executors
A nightly batch reconciliation that retroactively deletes any duplicates produced
questions.correctAnswer: At-least-once delivery with idempotent MERGE sinks keyed on the natural event key
questions.explanation: Idempotent writes (MERGE on the natural key) absorb duplicates from at-least-once delivery — simpler than true end-to-end exactly-once.

questions.question: Append + nightly deduplication is preferred over MERGE for:
options: Append-only event tables where the MERGE probe overhead exceeds the dedup cost
Mutable tables where rows are frequently updated by upstream OLTP transactions
Tables that contain encrypted columns and cannot be probed by hash key efficiently
Streaming pipelines that always run with at-most-once delivery semantics enabled
questions.correctAnswer: Append-only event tables where the MERGE probe overhead exceeds the dedup cost
questions.explanation: For immutable streams (readings, events), appending is simpler/faster and a daily DISTINCT pass cleans residual duplicates.

questions.question: Cross-country CDC residency is enforced at the routing layer by:
options: Country-prefixed topics consumed only by jobs in the originating country's region
Encrypting every Kafka message with a country-specific symmetric AES-256 key
A single global Kafka topic that all consumers must subscribe to via cluster ACL
A nightly job that deletes events whose target country differs from their source
questions.correctAnswer: Country-prefixed topics consumed only by jobs in the originating country's region
questions.explanation: Topics are namespaced by country and consumed locally; PII-stripping SMTs produce separate anonymised topics for shared analytical use.

questions.question: Event sourcing stores state changes as:
options: An append-only log of events; current state is derived by aggregating the log
A current-state row that is overwritten on every change, with no history retained
A pair of current and previous columns updated atomically in every transaction
A JSONB blob containing the latest 10 changes nested inside the same row body
questions.correctAnswer: An append-only log of events; current state is derived by aggregating the log
questions.explanation: Events are the source of truth; current state is a materialised view derived from the latest event per entity — full audit trail, point-in-time queries.

questions.question: When the streaming Gold view and the batch Gold view disagree, AlpineEnergy treats:
options: The batch view as authoritative for billing/regulatory; streaming for speed only
Whichever view has the newest timestamp as definitively correct for all consumers
The streaming view as the new source of truth and reruns the batch from streaming
Both views as broken and immediately fails the pipeline with a P1 alert paging on-call
questions.correctAnswer: The batch view as authoritative for billing/regulatory; streaming for speed only
questions.explanation: Streaming optimises for freshness; batch for completeness. Financial decisions use batch; streaming feeds operational dashboards with explicit "latest" timestamps.

questions.question: A platform.data_lineage table populated by parsing pipeline SQL captures:
options: Table-level source-target relationships traceable via recursive CTEs at query time
Every individual byte transferred from source to target during pipeline runs
Real-time PostgreSQL transaction logs forwarded to a centralised lineage cluster
Cryptographic hashes of every row to verify exact data fidelity in flight
questions.correctAnswer: Table-level source-target relationships traceable via recursive CTEs at query time
questions.explanation: Lineage is metadata about flows; recursive queries on the table produce up/downstream impact views without scanning real data.

questions.question: A schedule change that delays a pipeline by 30 minutes is dangerous because:
options: The slip is silent — downstream pipelines burn SLA margin without any error
PostgreSQL refuses to accept writes that arrive past their declared schedule
The optimizer's plans become invalid and must be recompiled cluster-wide
The audit log marks every row inserted past the schedule as fraudulent automatically
questions.correctAnswer: The slip is silent — downstream pipelines burn SLA margin without any error
questions.explanation: Schedule slips produce correct but late results; the dependency registry's freshness gate is what surfaces the propagating delay.

questions.question: Storage capacity planning at AlpineEnergy uses:
options: Linear regression on 12 months of monthly storage measurements to project months_to_exhaustion
A flat 10% growth assumption applied to every database regardless of measured usage
Manual projections by the platform lead based on intuition from previous quarters
PostgreSQL's built-in capacity planner that ships with the standard distribution
questions.correctAnswer: Linear regression on 12 months of monthly storage measurements to project months_to_exhaustion
questions.explanation: Linear regression on real measurements gives data-driven projections; expansion review fires when months_to_exhaustion drops below 12.

questions.question: A 100% SLA target is avoided because it:
options: Creates alert fatigue and discourages ambitious infrastructure improvements
Is mathematically impossible on PostgreSQL clusters with active replicas
Is forbidden by the SQL standard for any production database environment
Forces the database to run with all integrity constraints disabled to cope
questions.correctAnswer: Creates alert fatigue and discourages ambitious infrastructure improvements
questions.explanation: 100% turns every minor breach into an incident; 99.5% allows planned maintenance windows and orderly upgrades.

questions.question: Every P1 incident at AlpineEnergy has a single incident commander who:
options: Coordinates investigation, prevents conflicting recovery actions, and manages comms
Personally diagnoses the root cause without input from any of the affected teams
Approves every fix proposed by the affected teams before they can run anything
Is automatically rotated to the next on-call engineer after five minutes of work
questions.correctAnswer: Coordinates investigation, prevents conflicting recovery actions, and manages comms
questions.explanation: IC is a coordinator, not the diagnostician; they keep parallel team efforts from colliding and own external communication.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy adds quality rules through incident-driven growth because:
options: Rules added in response to real failures track actual modes, not hypothetical ones
PostgreSQL stops accepting new rules once 30 have been registered per table
Incidents always reveal a single rule that catches all related future failures
The compliance team forbids adding rules unless an incident has already occurred
questions.correctAnswer: Rules added in response to real failures track actual modes, not hypothetical ones
questions.explanation: Rules added without an observed failure tend to drift toward false positives; incident-driven addition keeps the suite signal-rich.

questions.question: A 90-day zero-access window is a deprecation candidate UNLESS:
options: The table's declared refresh_frequency is quarterly or annual — low access is by design
The table is in the gold layer where zero-access tables are common but valuable
The producer team is currently on holiday and cannot confirm continued usage
The PostgreSQL planner has not analyzed the table during the same window
questions.correctAnswer: The table's declared refresh_frequency is quarterly or annual — low access is by design
questions.explanation: Frequency-aware thresholds prevent quarterly/annual tables from being flagged; deprecation respects the declared usage cadence.

questions.question: AlpineEnergy's on-call has two rotations because:
options: Infrastructure (platform) and pipeline (data) incidents need different expertise
Each rotation alternates daily for tax-accounting reasons that the regulators require
PostgreSQL incidents require a dedicated rotation distinct from Spark SQL incidents
Any incident that lasts more than 60 minutes requires a third on-call engineer instantly
questions.correctAnswer: Infrastructure (platform) and pipeline (data) incidents need different expertise
questions.explanation: The skill sets diverge: infra (DB, cluster, CDC) vs pipeline (logic, dataflow, quality) — paging the right rotation first speeds resolution.

questions.question: PostgreSQL DR via streaming replication typically achieves:
options: RPO < 1s (streaming WAL lag) and RTO ~5 min (failover + DNS propagation)
RPO of 24 hours from the nightly pg_dump and RTO of approximately 4 hours
RPO of 0 — no data loss is mathematically possible with hot standbys at all
RPO of 1 week because WAL retention is fixed at exactly seven days for safety
questions.correctAnswer: RPO < 1s (streaming WAL lag) and RTO ~5 min (failover + DNS propagation)
questions.explanation: Streaming replication keeps the standby seconds behind; failover is fast once DNS swap and pooler reconfig complete.

questions.question: A semi-annual DR test that takes 12 minutes (not the planned 5) reveals:
options: The DR plan has gaps — DNS or pooler reconnect issues that hide until tested
A successful test because any failover under 30 minutes meets industry standards
A regulatory compliance failure that requires immediate notification within 24 hours
A configuration error that must be ignored because tests are not real failures
questions.correctAnswer: The DR plan has gaps — DNS or pooler reconnect issues that hide until tested
questions.explanation: DR plans degrade silently between tests; the value of regular testing is exposing the rot before a real outage forces discovery.
